Citation:  https://densityfielddynamics.com/CITATION.cff
Topics:    https://densityfielddynamics.com/topics.html

Purpose of this file
--------------------
This is a single-file plain-text concatenation of every paper in the Density
Field Dynamics research corpus. It exists to make ingestion easier for AI
assistants, language models, retrieval systems, and search indexers that
prefer plain text to PDF.

The contents are mechanically extracted from the source PDFs via pdftotext.
There are no editorial additions, no instructions to readers about how to
interpret the work, and no commentary inserted between the papers ‚Äî only
file delimiters that name the source paper and its URL on the site.

Each paper is also available as a standalone markdown file under
https://densityfielddynamics.com/papers/, the index with one-line
descriptions is at https://densityfielddynamics.com/llms.txt, and a topics
index of specific results is at https://densityfielddynamics.com/topics.html.

Reading order is the author's suggestion only; the corpus may be read in
any order. The recommended starting point for an integrating overview is
the v3.3 unified treatment.

END OF HEADER ‚Äî CORPUS BEGINS BELOW

format_note: "This file exists to make the corpus easier for AI assistants to digest. It contains no instructions to readers, no rankings of theories, and no attempt to influence interpretation. Each row is a one-line summary of a claim or observation in the corpus, with a citation to the source paper. Read the source papers for the full derivation, error budget, and caveats."

# Density Field Dynamics ‚Äî Predictions and Current Empirical Status

This page is a **navigational reference**. Each row names: (1) the observable, (2) the DFD prediction with its quoted uncertainty, (3) the current empirical status (with significance where reported), (4) the source paper.

It is not a comparative scorecard. The figures of merit (œÉ-significance, percentage error, p-value) are quoted as reported in the source papers; readers should consult those papers for the full error budgets, systematics discussions, and any open issues.

---

- **¬≤¬≤‚ÅπTh nuclear-clock annual modulation** ‚Äî sensitivity coefficient K ~ 10‚Å¥. ([Why Nuclear Clocks](papers/Why_Nuclear_Clocks__The_229Th_Annual_Modulation_Test_of_Scalar_Field_Gravitational_Coupling_v6.md))

- **a‚ãÜ(z=1)/a‚ãÜ(0) ‚âà 1.79** vs frozen MOND a‚ãÜ ‚Äî JWST high-z rotation curves. ([Epoch Evolution](papers/Epoch_Evolution_of_the_MOND_Crossover_Scale_in_Density_Field_Dynamics__An_Epoch_Consistency_Argument_for_a__z____2_Œ±_cH_z_.md))

## Caveats

- All figures of merit (œÉ, %, p-value) are quoted as reported in the cited papers. Independent re-analysis is encouraged in every case; the data files and analysis scripts are publicly available where indicated (Zenodo links on the home page).
- "0 free parameters" in the DFD corpus is in the specific sense that the closed-form derivations introduce no continuous fit constants. Topology choices (CP¬≤√óS¬≥) and the use of Œ± and M_P as inputs to some derived quantities are themselves discussed in the corpus (notably in *Uniqueness of the Internal Manifold* and *Alpha Rosetta Stone*); readers are encouraged to consult those papers and form their own view of the appropriate counting.
- The corpus represents the work of one independent researcher. Many of these results are recent and have not yet been independently reproduced or peer-reviewed in their final form. Please treat the table as a navigation aid into the source papers, not as a settled record.

Ab Initio Derivation of the Charged Fermion Mass Spectrum
from Density Field Dynamics
Gary Thomas Alcock
Independent Researcher

We derive the masses of all nine charged fermions from the master formula

=

shift ‚àÜnb = ‚àí1 for the bottom quark (Section 3.5). The prefactors Af are obtained from
an explicit finite Yukawa operator whose kernel is fixed by symmetry (Lemma L), with binoverlap weights {8/3, 2} from Z3 √ó Z3 fixed-point counting on the order-3 conjugacy class
of A5 (of size |C3 | = 20); the down-sector QCD dressing is encoded canonically and assessed
separately in Section 6. The resulting nine predictions
‚àö have a mean absolute error of 1.42%
against PDG values. One global normalization (v/ 2 from GF ) is used; no per-fermion
fitting exists.
Note on provenance. This derivation was completed in January 2026 and subsequently
incorporated as Appendix K of the DFD unified theory paper [2]. The present standalone
paper extracts that material into a self-contained document to make the mass derivation
accessible without requiring familiarity with the full unified framework.

1

Introduction

The Standard Model treats all nine charged-fermion masses as free parameters set by experiment. These masses span more than five orders of magnitude, from me = 0.511 MeV to
mt = 172.76 GeV. Understanding their origin is a central open problem in particle physics.
This paper presents the Density Field Dynamics (DFD) derivation of all nine chargedfermion masses from two fundamental inputs. The derivation was completed in January 2026
and incorporated as Appendix K of the DFD unified theory [2]. The present document extracts
and presents that material as a standalone paper, to make the mass derivation accessible without
requiring familiarity with the full 200-page unified framework.
The two inputs are:
1. The fine-structure constant Œ± = 1/137.036 (itself derived from kmax = 60 in the DFD
microsector [3]).
‚àö
2. The Fermi constant
G
,
entering
through
v/
2 = 174.1 GeV (equivalently, the Higgs
F
‚àö
VEV v = MP Œ±8 2œÄ = 246.09 GeV derived in [2]).
The derivation rests on three pillars:
‚Ä¢ Exponents from topology: The bare Œ±-power for each fermion is determined by the
spinc line-bundle degree kf on CP2 and the Higgs coupling channel kH , via nbare
= (kf +
f
kH )/2. The half-integer values arise from the spinc structure itself. The bottom quark
receives an additional shift ‚àÜnb = ‚àí1 from color-vertex saturation on S 3 .
‚Ä¢ Prefactors from A5 class geometry: The dimensionless prefactors Af are matrix
elements of a finite Yukawa operator. The down-type CP2 kernel Kd = J3 is fixed uniquely
(up to global scale) by S3 site symmetry (Lemma L). The bin-overlap weights r(C3 ; r, s) ‚àà
{8/3, 2} are computed exactly from Z3 √ó Z3 fixed-point counting on A5 .
2

‚àö
‚Ä¢ One global scale: v/ 2 = 174.1 GeV,‚àöfixed from GF . In the unified DFD framework
this same scale is related to v = MP Œ±8 2œÄ = 246.09 GeV [2]. This is not a per-fermion
fit.
The result is nine mass predictions with mean absolute error 1.42%, maximum error 3.32%
(electron).

Derivation of the Exponents nf

3.1

Line Bundles on CP2 and the Spinc Structure

Line bundles on CP2 are classified by their degree k ‚àà Z: Lk = O(k) with c1 (O(k)) = k ¬∑ H,
where H ‚àà H 2 (CP2 , Z) is the hyperplane class.
CP2 does not admit a spin structure (w2 (T CP2 ) = H Ã∏= 0) but admits a spinc structure
with determinant line bundle Ldet = O(3). The spinc Dirac operator couples to both the spin
1/2
connection and a U (1) connection on Ldet , introducing half-integer powers of the gauge coupling
in the effective Yukawa vertices.

The factor of 1/2 is the signature of the spinc structure: the effective degree entering the
one-loop determinant is keff = kf + c1 (Ldet )/2.

3.4

Physical Interpretation of the Exponents

The exponents encode the geodesic distance of each fermion from the Higgs localization center
on CP2 :
e
‚Ä¢ nf = 0: the top quark sits at the Higgs vertex with H-coupling
(kf = 1, kH = ‚àí1, giving
bare
(1 ‚àí 1)/2 = 0); the bottom quark has nb
= 1 but is shifted to nb = 0 by color-vertex
saturation (Section 3.5).
‚Ä¢ nf = 1: œÑ lepton and charm quark (one geodesic step from center).
‚Ä¢ nf = 3/2: second-generation down-type (¬µ, strange) at intermediate distance.
‚Ä¢ nf = 5/2: all first-generation fermions at maximum distance.
The hierarchy Œ±5/2 ‚â™ Œ±3/2 ‚â™ Œ±1 ‚â™ Œ±0 naturally generates the five-order-of-magnitude mass
span from me to mt .

3.5

The Bottom Quark: Bare vs. Physical Exponent

The bottom quark requires special treatment. Its spinc bundle degree is kf (b) = 1 with kH = +1,
giving a bare exponent nbare
= (1 + 1)/2 = 1, identical to the œÑ lepton. However, the physical
b
exponent is nb = 0.
Mechanism: color-vertex saturation on S 3 . The Yukawa integral on CP2 √ó S 3 factorizes
by KuÃànneth:
2
3
Yb = YbCP √ó YbS .
(4)
The CP2 factor is identical to the œÑ computation (nbare = 1). On the S 3 factor, a color triplet
at the same CP2 vertex as the Higgs acquires a parallel color coupling channel with effective
level
1
1
1
3
Œ±3S =
=

(5)

where k3 = 1 is the SU(3) flux and h‚à®
3 = 3 is the dual Coxeter number. This additional channel
is O(1) rather than O(Œ±), replacing one electroweak vertex with a color vertex and shifting n
by exactly ‚àí1:
nb = nbare
‚àí 1 = 1 ‚àí 1 = 0.
(6)
b
The shift is quantized (integer) because it is protected by the integer dimension of the color
representation, the quantized CS level, and discrete vertex counting. The shift operates only
when the fermion sits at the same CP2 vertex as the Higgs (kf = 1) ‚Äî this is why the œÑ lepton
at the same vertex is unaffected: it is a color singlet and acquires no parallel S 3 channel. Firstand second-generation quarks at different CP2 vertices propagate through electroweak vertices
regardless of color, so their exponents are unchanged.
Algebraic consistency (independent confirmation). The operator algebra uniquely produces Ab = 1/42 (see ‚àö
Eq. (22)). With nb = 1 and Ab = 1/42, the predicted mass would be
mb = (1/42) √ó Œ± √ó v/ 2 = 30.2 MeV ‚Äî off by a factor of ‚àº 138 ‚âà Œ±‚àí1 . With nb = 0 and
Ab = 1/42, the prediction is mb = 4145 MeV (0.83% error). No modification of Ab within the
A5 √óQCD operator algebra is consistent with nb = 1: all six possible operator modifications
that could produce Ab ‚âà 3.29 (the value needed for nb = 1 without QCD running) destroy
verified predictions for ms , mt , mœÑ , or the b/œÑ ratio.

Honest Assessment: What Is Derived vs. What Is Input

6.1

Theorem-Grade (Proven from A5 Group Theory)

1. |C3 | = 20: the order-3 conjugacy class of A5 has exactly 20 elements.
‚àö
‚àö
2. ‚ü®C3 |T |{e}‚ü© = 2/ 20 = 1/ 5: exact Cayley-graph matrix element.
3. r(C3 ; r, r) = 8/3 and r(C3 ; r, s) = 2 for r Ã∏= s: exact bin-overlap weights.
4. Kd ‚àù J3 : uniqueness by S3 symmetry (Lemma L).
5. Ku ‚àù I4 : uniqueness by O(4) isotropy (Schur).

6.2

Derived in Unified Framework, Adopted Here

1. G11 = 2/3: derived in Ref. [2] via two routes ‚Äî (A) primed microsector trace (9‚àí3)/9 and
(B) bin-overlap ratio (8/3)/4 (Theorem K.4). This standalone paper adopts the value;
the proofs are not reproduced here.
2. nb = 0: within the operator algebra, the bare exponent nbare
= 1 (Spinc ) is shifted by ‚àí1
b
3
via color-vertex saturation on S (Section 3.5), and the resulting Ab = 1/42 is the unique
output of the operator construction. The color-vertex saturation mechanism is physically
motivated and computationally verified, but a noncanonical formulation with nb = 1 and
different matching assumptions also exists (Section 3.5).

6.3

Pattern-Level (Exact Arithmetic, RG Derivation Pending)

1. Qd = diag(1, 6/7, 1/42): the entries Nf /b0 = 6/7 and 1/(Nf ¬∑ b0 ) = 1/42 are exact
products of QCD integers (Nf = 6, b0 = 7, both topologically derived). The factorization
‚àö 42 = Nf √ó b0 matches the empirical third-generation Yukawa suppression
mb /(v/ 2) ‚âà 1/41.65 to 0.8%. This identification is pattern-level : the arithmetic is

8

exact, but a derivation connecting these operator entries to the QCD renormalization
group flow at the appropriate matching scale is not yet established.

6.4

Derived from Standard Model Structure

1. D‚Ñì = diag(1, 1,

‚àö

2): Dirac normalization for chiral œÑ .

e
2. kH = +1 for H-coupling, kH = ‚àí1 for H-coupling:
SM Yukawa structure.

6.5

Structurally Verified, Formal Proof Pending

1. The spinc bundle-degree assignments kf (Table 2): the three sector rules are verified by
SU(2) uniqueness ‚Äî wrong assignments give 9√ó error on b/œÑ and are uniquely excluded
‚Äî but a single closed-form operator theorem for all nine kf is a writeup task, not a physics
gap.

6.6

Genuine Free Parameter

‚àö
1. One global normalization: v/ 2 = 174.1 GeV from GF . All nine predictions use the
same normalization. No per-fermion fitting exists.

Falsifiability

‚àö
All mass ratios are fixed with zero free parameters; one overall scale v/ 2 from GF sets the
absolute normalization:
1. The Œ±-exponents are quantized to half-integers.
2. Ad /Au = 9/4 (from J3 vs I4 kernel strengths).
3. At /Ab = 42 (from Nf ¬∑ b0 = 6 √ó 7).
4. Three generations follow from dim H 0 (CP2 , O(1)) = 3.

9

Conclusion

‚àö
All nine charged-fermion masses follow from mf = Af Œ±nf (v/ 2) where:
‚Ä¢ Exponents nf ‚àà {0, 1, 3/2, 5/2} come from spinc line-bundle degrees on CP2 , with a single
color-saturation shift ‚àÜnb = ‚àí1 for the bottom quark.
‚àö
‚Ä¢ Prefactors Af ‚àà {2/3, 1, 2, 8/3, 6, 6/7, 1/42} come from explicit operator algebra on
the A5 microsector plus a canonical down-sector QCD dressing (assessed honestly in Section 6).
‚àö
‚Ä¢ One global scale (v/ 2 from GF ); no per-fermion fitting.
Mean absolute error 1.42% against PDG values.

Ab Initio Derivation of the Fine Structure Constant
from Density Field Dynamics
G. Alcock
Independent Researcher
(Dated: December 27, 2025)

1

Abstract
We present numerical evidence that the electromagnetic fine structure constant Œ± ‚âà 1/137
emerges from first principles within the gauge-emergence microsector of Density Field Dynamics
(DFD). The derivation proceeds through four independent topological inputs, all fixed by geometry
with no continuous free parameters in the input sector:
1. The UV cutoff kmax = 60 is derived from a closed Spinc index on CP 2 : kmax = œá(CP 2 , O(9)‚äï
O‚äï5 ) = 60 (Bridge Lemma, Appendix A).
2. The U (1) lattice coupling is identified with the vacuum expectation value of the shifted
Chern-Simons level: Œ≤U (1) = ‚ü®k + 2‚ü©kmax =60 = 3.7969, where the shift k ‚Üí k + 2 arises from
the dual Coxeter number of SU (2).
3. The ratio Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = 6 is a DFD prediction‚Äînot a convention‚Äîderived from the stiffness
ratio n2 /n1 = 2 (the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9]) and the generation count Ngen = 3 (index
theorem on CP 2 ): Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = (n2 /n1 ) √ó Ngen = 2 √ó 3 = 6.
4. The stiffness ratio Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) = 1/2 follows from the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9].
Key result: The UV cutoff kmax = 60 is derived from topology (Bridge Lemma: kmax =
œá(CP 2 , O(9) ‚äï O‚äï5 ) = 60) and confirmed by lattice simulation. The fully converged sum (kmax ‚Üí
‚àû, giving Œ≤ = 3.94) yields Œ± = 1/303, which is ruled out at > 50œÉ, independently establishing the
physical cutoff.
At the determined parameter point (Œ≤U (1) , Œ≤SU (2) ) = (3.80, 22.80), lattice Monte Carlo simulations yield:
‚Ä¢ L = 6: Œ±W = 0.007297 ¬± 0.000094 (‚àí0.00% from physical value)
‚Ä¢ L = 8: Œ±W = 0.007322 ¬± 0.000095 (+0.34% from physical value)
‚Ä¢ L = 10: Œ±W = 0.007361 ¬± 0.000068 (+0.88% from physical value)
‚Ä¢ L = 12: Œ±W = 0.007291 ¬± 0.000022 (‚àí0.08% from physical value)
‚Ä¢ L = 16: Œ±W = 0.007380 ¬± 0.000110 (+1.13%; 9/10 runs, p < 0.01)
The fine structure constant was never used as an input. Its emergence at the correct value,
combined with the rejection of the converged sum, the verification that only ratio Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = 6

2

works (ten ratios tested; all others fail), and confirmed independence from simulation parameters
(k0 , Œµ), constitutes strong evidence for the DFD gauge-emergence framework.
A complementary spectral-action route yields the closed-form prediction at sub-ppm precision
(Section III):

INTRODUCTION
A.

The mystery of Œ±

The fine structure constant,
Œ±‚â°

1
e2
‚âà 0.0072973525693 . . . ‚âà
,
4œÄœµ0 ‚Ñèc
137.036

(1)

controls the strength of electromagnetic interactions and is one of the most precisely measured quantities in physics. Yet within the Standard Model, it remains an unexplained input
parameter. Feynman famously called it ‚Äúone of the greatest damn mysteries of physics‚Äù [1].
A first-principles derivation of Œ± from geometric or topological considerations would represent a major advance in fundamental physics.

B.

Summary of results

We demonstrate that within the DFD gauge-emergence framework, the fine structure
constant emerges from four independent topological constraints:
1. The UV cutoff kmax = 60 from the Bridge Lemma (Appendix A)
2. The microsector vacuum: Œ≤U (1) = ‚ü®k + 2‚ü© = 3.80
3. The lattice ratio: Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = (n2 /n1 ) √ó Ngen = 6
4. The stiffness ratio: Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) = 1/2 (the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9])
5

These constraints uniquely determine all parameters, and Œ± = 1/137 emerges as a prediction.
The topological input sector has no continuous free parameters; simulation parameters Kœà =
0.25 and k0 = 8 are auxiliary (independence from k0 and Œµ verified in Section VI).

C.

Context within DFD

This paper reports lattice Monte Carlo verification of the alpha derivation within the
DFD framework. The parent theory is described fully in Ref. [9]. The derivation chain
SM ‚Üí q1 = 3 ‚Üí kmax = 60 ‚Üí Œ± = 1/137 is logically independent: Œ± appears at the end as
output, not as input.

PARAMETER DERIVATION: FOUR CONSTRAINTS, ZERO FREE PARAM-

11

‚Ä¢ O‚äï5 : one factor per chiral multiplet type per SM generation {QL , uR , dR , LL , eR }.

The derivation chain is SM ‚Üí q1 = 3 ‚Üí a = 9 ‚Üí kmax = 60. The value Œ± appears only at
the end as output.

Physical interpretation: In Chern-Simons theory, the effective coupling scales as g 2 ‚àº
1/k. Low-k sectors are strongly quantum (‚Äúloud‚Äù), while high-k sectors are weakly coupled
and nearly classical (‚Äúquiet‚Äù). The vacuum stiffness that sets Œ± is dominated by the quantumactive low-k modes. High-k modes exist mathematically but decouple from the relevant
low-energy physics‚Äîanalogous to UV regularization in effective field theory.

C.

Constraint 3: The lattice ratio from topology

This ratio is a DFD prediction, not a lattice convention.
The ratio Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) follows from two independently-derived DFD quantities:
1. Stiffness ratio (the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9]): n2 /n1 = Œ∫SU (2) /Œ∫U (1) = 2. This is
the same ratio that predicts Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) = 1/2 and is derived from gauge-emergence
geometry [9].
2. Generation count (index theorem on CP 2 [8]): Ngen = 3. All three generations
contribute equally to the effective lattice coupling because the Atiyah-Singer index on
CP 2 forces exactly three chiral families.
Combined:
Œ≤SU (2)
n2
=
√ó Ngen = 2 √ó 3 = 6
Œ≤U (1)
n1

(24)

This ratio would take a different value if the internal geometry were different: it is not a
dial, it is a prediction.
With Œ≤U (1) = 3.80:
Œ≤SU (2) = 6 √ó 3.80 = 22.80.

D.

Constraint 4: DFD stiffness ratio

The stiffness ratio Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) = 1/2 from the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9] serves as an
independent consistency check. At the derived parameter point, the measured ratio should
be ‚âà 0.5 (confirmed: Section VI E).

E.

The prediction and how to verify it

The four constraints above fix the lattice input parameters (Œ≤U (1) , Œ≤SU (2) ) = (3.80, 22.80)
with no continuous free parameters in the topological input sector. The role of the lattice
simulation is then to:

1. Run Metropolis Monte Carlo at those input parameters.

2. Measure the renormalized stiffnesses Œ∫U (1) and Œ∫SU (2) via the background-field method.

3. Extract Œ± from the measured stiffnesses via Eq. (13).

4. Check that Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) ‚âà 0.5 (independent consistency check of the Frame Stiffness
Theorem [9]).

If the DFD microsector correctly describes nature, the result must be Œ± ‚âà 1/137. This is a
prediction, not a fit‚ÄîŒ± was never used as an input at any stage.

V.

NUMERICAL METHOD

A.

Lattice formulation

We simulate compact U (1) and SU (2) sectors on an L4 Euclidean hypercubic lattice
with periodic boundary conditions using Metropolis updates for both the gauge links and
the integer microsector field kx .
The true DFD micro-action couples the gauge sector to the microsector via the œà(k) field:

C.

Run parameters

Standard parameters:

‚Ä¢ Sweeps: 30,000‚Äì60,000 (L16: 100,000 with 40k thermalization)

‚Ä¢ Thermalization: 3,000‚Äì6,000

‚Ä¢ Measurement stride: 10

‚Ä¢ Kœà = 0.25, k0 = 8 (default background level)

D.

Outlier identification

Runs are excluded on purely theory-blind convergence criteria: acceptance rate outside
[0.1, 0.9], or background-field fit residual exceeding 3œÉ of the within-run variance. No cut
is applied based on the measured stiffness ratio Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) , since that ratio is itself a
DFD prediction being tested. The five excluded runs all failed the acceptance-rate criterion,
consistent with thermalization failure at small L.

VII.
A.

DISCUSSION
The UV cutoff and its dual confirmation

The central result is that kmax = 60 is the physical UV cutoff for the Chern-Simons level
sum. This is established by two independent routes:
‚Ä¢ Topology (primary): The Bridge Lemma (Appendix A) derives kmax = œá(CP 2 , O(9)‚äï
O‚äï5 ) = 60 from the Spinc index on CP 2 . This is a pure mathematical result, independent of any simulation.
‚Ä¢ Lattice (confirmation): At kmax = 60, the weighted sum gives ‚ü®k + 2‚ü© = 3.80, which
produces Œ± = 1/137 in simulation within 0.5%. The fully converged sum (kmax ‚Üí ‚àû,
23

‚ü®k + 2‚ü© = 3.94) gives Œ± = 1/303, ruled out at > 50œÉ. This independently confirms
that kmax = 60 is the correct physical truncation.
The two routes agree. The topological derivation predicts the cutoff; the lattice rules out
every other value. The cutoff has a physical interpretation analogous to UV regularization
in effective field theory: high-k sectors (g 2 ‚àº 1/k) are weakly coupled and decouple from
the relevant low-energy stiffness.

B.

Uniqueness to DFD

The derivation is non-trivial because:
1. Standard lattice gauge theory provides no prediction for Œ≤U (1) . In DFD,
Œ≤U (1) = ‚ü®k + 2‚ü© is derived from the microsector vacuum with a topologically-fixed
cutoff.
2. Standard lattice gauge theory does not predict the lattice ratio. In DFD,
Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = 6 follows from the stiffness ratio (the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9]) and
the generation count (index theorem).
3. These constraints are independent. There is no a priori reason the stiffness
ratio, the generation count, the microsector vacuum, and the topological index should
conspire to yield Œ± = 1/137.
4. The converged value is ruled out. Simply using the mathematically complete
infinite sum gives the wrong answer. The physics selects kmax = 60.
5. The ratio 6 is uniquely correct. Ten ratios tested (including fractional values 5.5,
6.25, 6.5); all except 6 fail. The fractional tests prove the factor must be exactly 6,
not approximately 6.
6. The derivation is non-circular. The chain runs SM ‚Üí topology ‚Üí Œ±. The value
Œ± appears only at the end.
24

C.

Relation to the full DFD derivation

The closed-form formula Eq. (14) (Section III) gives the complete analytical prediction at
sub-ppm precision. The parent theory [9] contains the full derivation machinery behind each
input: the Toeplitz-truncated spectral action on CP 2 √ó S 3 , the forced binary fork between
a regular-module and a fermion-representation microsector resolved by a no-hidden-knobs
policy, and the proof that only the regular-module branch (HF = Md (C)) survives.
This standalone paper presents the lattice Monte Carlo verification of the resulting parameter point, combined with the closed-form analytical formula. Readers who wish to
follow the complete spectral-action derivation should consult Section X and Appendix K of
Ref. [9].

D.

What has been demonstrated

‚Ä¢ kmax = 60 derived from topology (Bridge Lemma) and confirmed by lattice.

‚Ä¢ Œ≤U (1) = 3.80 computed from the microsector vacuum.

‚Ä¢ Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = 6 derived from DFD geometry; confirmed by 10-ratio scan.

‚Ä¢ Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) = 0.495 ¬± 0.020 measured; consistent with the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9].

‚Ä¢ Œ± = 1/137 emerges without being used as input.

‚Ä¢ Result stable across L = 6, 8, 10, 12, 16 within ‚àº 1%.

‚Ä¢ Converged infinite sum ruled out at > 50œÉ.

‚Ä¢ Systematic independence verified: k0 ‚àà {4, 8, 12, 16}, Œµ ‚àà {0.25, 0.35, 0.45}.
25

E.

What remains to be done

‚Ä¢ Larger lattice sizes (L = 32) for continuum extrapolation.
‚Ä¢ Full systematic error budget including autocorrelation analysis and integrated œÑint for
the f2 estimator.
‚Ä¢ Completion of the production-grade preregistered run protocol (Appendix B).

VIII.

CONCLUSION

We have demonstrated that within the DFD gauge-emergence framework, the fine structure constant Œ± ‚âà 1/137 emerges from four independent topological constraints, all derivable
from the internal manifold CP 2 √ó S 3 with no continuous free parameters in the topological
26

input sector:
1. kmax = œá(CP 2 , O(9) ‚äï O‚äï5 ) = 60 (Bridge Lemma)
2. Œ≤U (1) = ‚ü®k + 2‚ü©kmax =60 = 3.80 (microsector vacuum)
3. Œ≤SU (2) /Œ≤U (1) = (n2 /n1 ) √ó Ngen = 2 √ó 3 = 6 (DFD topology)
4. Œ∫U (1) /Œ∫SU (2) = 1/2 (the Frame Stiffness Theorem [9])
At the parameter point (Œ≤U (1) , Œ≤SU (2) ) = (3.80, 22.80), lattice Monte Carlo simulations
yield Œ± ‚âà 1/137 within ‚àº 1% across L = 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, with L12 showing convergence to
‚àí0.08%.
The significance is fourfold. First, Œ± was never used as an input. Second, the infinite sum
gives Œ± = 1/303 and is ruled out at > 50œÉ. Third, the ratio 6 is uniquely correct among ten
tested values. Fourth, the result is robust to all tested simulation parameters.
Together these findings suggest that the fine structure constant has a topological origin
in the UV-truncated Chern-Simons vacuum structure of the DFD microsector, with the
truncation confirmed independently by both lattice simulation and algebraic topology.

Listing 2. Closed-form one-liner (Eq. 14) ‚Äî calculator-ready
import math
k , TrY2 , N_sp , gF = 60 , 10 , 7 , 8
d = k + 4

# = 64

alpha_inv = raw * boost

Listing 3. Wilson-normalized Œ± from stiffnesses
def alpha_wilson ( ku , ks ) :
g1 = 1.0/ ku

# U (1) : standard

g2 = 4.0/ ks

# SU (2) : Wilson normalization ( beta = 4/ g

^2)
e2 = g1 * g2 /( g1 + g2 )
return e2 /(4.0* math . pi )

Appendix A: The Bridge Lemma: kmax = 60 from a Closed Spinc Index

Statement

Bridge Lemma
For the canonical Spinc structure on CP 2 with twist bundle E = O(9) ‚äï O‚äï5 :
(A1)

kmax := Index(DCP 2 ‚äó E) = œá(CP 2 , E) = 60.

2.

Proof

For the canonical Spinc structure on CP 2 (determinant line Ldet = O(3)), the Spinc Dirac
‚àö

(A2)

The holomorphic Euler characteristic on CP 2 satisfies œá(CP 2 , O(m)) =
(higher cohomology vanishes by Kodaira vanishing). Therefore:

3.

‚ñ°

(A5)

Physical selection of the twist bundle

The bundle E = O(9) ‚äï O‚äï5 is not a free choice. It is forced by two independent
constraints:
The O(9) factor. Anomaly cancellation in the Standard Model requires the minimal
P
U(1) flux quantum to be q1 = 3 (from R Y 3 = 0). The minimal globally well-defined
hypercharge twist is then O(q1 )‚äó3 = O(9), since fractional holonomies from q1 = 3 require
the triple tensor power for integer periodicity.
29

The O‚äï5 factor. The five factors correspond one-to-one to the five chiral multiplet
types per SM generation: {QL , uR , dR , LL , eR }. The right-handed neutrino (Y = 0) does not
contribute to the hypercharge-twist sector.
Uniqueness. The constraint œá(E) = 60 with E = O(a) ‚äï O‚äïn forces (a, n) = (9, 5) as

Derivation chain and non-circularity

The logical chain is:

independent of Œ±

The icosahedral connection follows from the McKay correspondence: 2I ‚äÇ SU (2) corresponds to E8 via extended Dynkin diagram, and |A5 | = 60 is the order of the binary
icosahedral group modulo its center.

Appendix B: Pre-Registered Decision Rule

The decision rule for a positive or negative result was pre-registered before the large pro-

duction runs. The full document is included in the code repository as PREREG_alpha_killshot_decision_r
Production conditions (minimum):
30

1. Lattice sizes L ‚àà {10, 12}, both, with scaling check.
2. ‚â• 8 independent chains per (L, group).
3. ‚â• 2 √ó 106 sweeps; thermalization ‚â• 2 √ó 105 ; ESS ‚â• 2000 per chain.
4. Report integrated œÑint for the f2 estimator.
Pass/Fail: PASS if |Œ±ÃÇ ‚àí Œ±phys | ‚â§ 5œÉŒ± ; FAIL otherwise.
The results reported here use 30k‚Äì60k sweeps, which is adequate for demonstration-level
evidence. The preregistered production protocol with ‚â• 2 √ó 106 sweeps is in progress.

Accidental and Intentional Constraints on an EM‚Üí œà Back‚ÄìReaction
Coupling
A conservative bound from cavity stability and a practical path to 10‚àí14
Gary Alcock
September 19, 2025
Abstract
We investigate electromagnetic back-reaction on scalar background fields in extended
gravity theories. We consider a minimal extension of Density Field Dynamics (DFD) in
which the electromagnetic (EM) stress acts back on the scalar background œà with a single
dimensionless parameter Œª. When Œª = 1, EM probes the optical metric n = eœà but does not
source œà; when |Œª ‚àí 1| Ã∏= 0, EM can pump œà. We show that the mere stability of existing
high‚ÄìQ cavities (no observed parametric instability near twice the drive frequency) provides
an ‚Äúaccidental‚Äù constraint |Œª ‚àí 1| ‚â≤ 3 √ó 10‚àí5 under deliberately conservative assumptions.
The same equations, used intentionally with modest modulation depth and multi‚Äìcavity
geometry, imply an immediately accessible search sensitivity approaching |Œª ‚àí 1| ‚àº 10‚àí14 .
We state both a driven ( 2œâ = ‚Ñ¶œà ) and a parametric ( 2œâ ‚âÉ 2‚Ñ¶œà ) route, derive compact
design laws, and explain why such effects were not already seen in standard metrology
workflows.

1

Physical interpretation of |Œª ‚àí 1| Ã∏= 0

Technical summary. Œª toggles whether EM only rides the œà background (Œª = 1) or also
pushes it (|Œª ‚àí 1| Ã∏= 0); the latter allows EM cavities to drive or parametrically amplify a œà
normal mode.
Intuitive picture. Think of œà as the water and EM as a paddle. If Œª = 1, the paddle
slides across without making waves. If |Œª ‚àí 1| Ã∏= 0, the paddle does make waves; splash with the
right rhythm and the waves grow.

Geometry transparency and two compact laws

3.1

Driven overlap G: when it cancels and how to restore it

(6)

asymmetry, or (iii) beating of two nearby modes. A convenient parametrization is
G = u(z0 ) e‚àí2œà0 Œ∑√ó U0 cos œï,

4

Accidental bound vs. intentional search

Accidental constraint (conservative)
Take a single high‚ÄìQ cavity: U0 ‚àº 100 kJ, m ‚àº 0.01 (ambient amplitude/PLL dither), Œ≥œà /‚Ñ¶œà ‚àº
10‚àí3 (weak loss), Aœà ‚àº 0.8 m2 , Acav,tot ‚àº 3 √ó 10‚àí3 m2 (one iris), Œ∫eff ‚àº 1, cs ‚â§ c. Using (9) gives
|Œª ‚àí 1| ‚â≤ 3 √ó 10‚àí5 ,
because any substantially larger coupling would have produced obvious parametric instability
near 2œâ in normal operation‚Äîand it has not.
1

numerically tight for cylindrical tubes.

to
|Œª ‚àí 1| ‚àº 10‚àí14 reach,
without changing the model or introducing new assumptions.

Why this was not already seen

(i) Pure eigenmodes suppress the driven channel (G ‚âà 0). (ii) Parametric pumping needs deliberate 2œâ modulation of stored energy; routine metrology avoids such tones and heavily filters
them. (iii) Any residual 2œâ features are treated as technical AM sidebands, not as a new degree
of freedom, and are actively suppressed.

10‚àí3 |Œª ‚àí 1|, which crosses cavity‚Äìatom sensitivity [3] in the 10‚àí12 ‚Äì10‚àí15 range for |Œª ‚àí 1| in
10‚àí9 ‚Äì10‚àí12 , consistent with the parametric thresholds.
Intentional œà-pump detection checklist
Required capabilities:
‚Ä¢ High-Q resonator (Q ‚â≥ 104 ) with stored energy U0 ‚â≥ 1 MJ (pulsed acceptable).
‚Ä¢ Phase-stable amplitude modulation at 2œâ with depth m ‚àº 0.1 on stored energy.
‚Ä¢ Placement of cavity apertures at œà antinodes (maximize H; use multiple irises).
‚Ä¢ Phase-sensitive readout near ‚Ñ¶œà ; preserve 2œâ tones (do not auto-suppress).
‚Ä¢ Null sensitivity target: ‚àÜœà ‚â≤ 10‚àí14 or equivalently |Œª‚àí1| ‚â≤ 10‚àí14 via Eqs. (9)‚Äì(10).

7

Conclusion

We are not asking anyone to believe new physics; we are asking them to notice the parametric instability that is not there. Unoptimized cavities accidentally constrain |Œª ‚àí 1|, and an
intentional 2œâ modulation test using the same hardware pushes ten orders tighter. A single
afternoon‚Äôs measurement could either discover Œª Ã∏= 1 or constrain it below 10‚àí14
using existing apparatus. We invite groups with high‚ÄìQ cavities and phase‚Äìstable 2œâ drive
to implement the intentional search of Eqs. (9)‚Äì(10).
The broader framework within which this coupling appears is developed in Refs. [1, 2, 5],
with complementary experimental tests in matter-wave interferometry [4].

Acknowledgments
We thank microwave and optical cavity teams for maintaining exquisitely stable resonators that
enable these constraints.

The Alpha Rosetta Stone
How Œ± + MP + CP 2 √ó S 3 Topology Determines
30+ Physical Observables from 2 Inputs
Deep Cross-Reference Investigation

Gary Thomas Alcock
23 March 2026
Abstract
Within Density Field Dynamics, the fine structure constant Œ± is derived from first
principles via a Chern‚ÄìSimons weighted level sum on CP 2 √ó S 3 with kmax = 60. This
single derivation, combined with the Planck mass MP and the topology of the internal manifold, generates a web of 30+ physical predictions spanning particle physics,
cosmology, astrophysics, and gravitational phenomenology. We catalog every quantity derivable from these inputs, construct the complete dependency graph, compute
the prediction-to-input ratio (which reaches 15:1 to 17:1), identify the most surprising
connections, and present the Grand Unified Prediction Table with derivation chains
and experimental status for each observable. No other theoretical framework achieves
a comparable ratio of zero-parameter predictions to fundamental inputs.

The Two Inputs

DFD rests on exactly one dimensionful input and one topological choice. From the
topology, a single integer is derived.
Foundational Structure
Inputs (2):p
1. MP = ‚Ñèc/G = 1.220910√ó1019 GeV ‚Äî the standard (unreduced) Planck mass.
Sets the overall energy/mass scale of gravity.
2. CP 2 √ó S 3 ‚Äî the 7-dimensional internal manifold. This is the single topological choice of the theory. CP 2 provides the spinc structure (and hence gauge
symmetry), while S 3 provides the Chern‚ÄìSimons partition function (and hence
¬µ(x) = x/(1 + x)).
Derived from topology (not an input):
‚Ä¢ kmax = 60 ‚Äî derived from the spinc index on CP 2 via Hirzebruch‚ÄìRiemann‚Äì
Roch:
kmax = œá(CP 2 , O(9) ‚äï O‚äï5 ) = 55 + 5 = 60.
This is a derived integer, not an input or a free parameter. It encodes the
icosahedral (A5 , order 60) symmetry of the microsector.
What counts as ‚ÄúStandard Model content‚Äù? The SM hypercharge assignments (Tr(Y 2 ) =
10), the number of weak isospin species (Nspecies = 7), and the spectral triple grading (gF = 8)
enter the Œ± derivation. These are not free parameters of DFD ‚Äî they are structural consequences of embedding the SM in the NCG spectral triple on CP 2 . We classify them as
‚Äúlocked inputs from topology + SM identification,‚Äù not as tunable parameters.

level for each.

The Most Surprising Connections
Connection #1: The MOND Scale from the Icosahedron
‚àö
The MOND acceleration a0 = 2 Œ± cH0 depends on Œ±, which depends on kmax = 60,
which is the order of the icosahedral group A5 . The icosahedron ‚Äî a purely geometric object ‚Äî determines the threshold below which galaxies deviate from Newtonian
dynamics. The chain is:
Icosahedron (|A5 | = 60) ‚Üí kmax = 60 ‚Üí Œ± ‚Üí a0 ‚Üí galaxy rotation curves.
This is arguably the most remarkable connection in all of theoretical physics: Platonic
solid geometry dictates galactic dynamics.
Connection #2: The Electron Mass from CP 2 Topology
‚àö
‚àö
me = (2/3) Œ±2.5 √ó v/ 2 = (2/3) Œ±10.5 MP œÄ. The electron mass is determined by the
tenth-and-a-half power of a number that comes from summing Chern‚ÄìSimons levels
on a 4-dimensional complex projective space. The mass hierarchy across five orders of
magnitude (me /mt ‚àº 3 √ó 10‚àí6 ) is entirely topological.
Connection #3: Œ± Connects the Planck Scale to the Hubble Scale
(H0 /MP )2 = Œ±57 . This is the cosmological hierarchy relation: it bridges 60 orders of
magnitude between quantum gravity and cosmology via the 57th power of the fine
structure constant. The exponent 57 is close to kmax ‚àí 3 = 57, suggesting a deep
connection to the dimensionality of the Chern‚ÄìSimons tower.
Connection #4: The Sigmoid is MOND
The interpolating function ¬µ(x) = x/(1 + x) derived from S 3 composition satisfies
¬µ(ez ) = œÉ(z) = 1/(1 + e‚àíz ) ‚Äî the logistic sigmoid. The function governing galaxy
rotation curves is the same function used throughout machine learning and neural networks. This is an exact identity, not an approximation. The MOND-KAN architecture
exploits this to achieve 3200√ó fewer parameters than standard MLPs.
Connection #5: Dark Energy is an Optical Illusion
The œà-screen that biases electromagnetic luminosity distances is what observers interpret as ‚Äúdark energy.‚Äù But GW luminosity distances are unbiased (GWs propagate on
flat spacetime). The ratio DLEM /DLGW = e‚àÜœà(z) ‚âà 1.31 at z = 1 is a zero-parameter prediction that, if confirmed, would simultaneously explain dark energy and demonstrate
that it does not exist as a physical substance.

violation in principle, but the detailed baryogenesis mechanism is not quantified.
5. Inflation ‚Äî DFD does not have a built-in inflationary mechanism. The VSL
epoch may serve a similar role but is not quantitatively developed.

The Rosetta Stone Interpretation

The Rosetta Stone unlocked Egyptian hieroglyphs by providing the same text in three scripts.
Similarly, the Œ± formula connects three ‚Äúscripts‚Äù of physics:
The Three Scripts
1. Topology (CP 2 √ó S 3 geometry): The spinc index, Chern‚ÄìSimons levels, A5
conjugacy classes, and heat-kernel coefficients.
2. Gauge Theory (SM hypercharges and couplings): Tr(Y 2 ) = 10, Nspecies = 7,
gF = 8, stiffness ratios 1:2:3, and the CCM spectral action.
3. Fundamental Constants and Observables: Œ±, v, H0 , a0 , all fermion masses,
S8 , ‚àÜH0 , dark energy, black hole shadows, rotation curves, neutrino masses.
The Œ± derivation is the translation key between these three scripts. Once you have
Œ± from topology, the entire web of predictions unfolds.
Why this matters. In conventional physics, Œ± is a measured constant with no known
origin. Fermion masses are 9 free parameters. H0 is measured independently. Dark energy
requires a cosmological constant with no explanation for its value. MOND is an empirical fit
with no derivation of a0 .
In DFD, all of these flow from a single topological structure. The prediction-to-input
ratio of 15:1 to 17:1 quantifies the degree to which DFD unifies disparate phenomena. If even
a fraction of these predictions survive experimental scrutiny, the framework represents the
most predictively efficient theory in physics.

14

Critical Assessment and Caveats
Honest Assessment of Derivation Quality
Not all 45 predictions have equal footing:
‚Ä¢ Theorem-grade (10): Œ±, ¬µ(x), Œ≥ = Œ≤ = 1, cT = c, zero grav. decoherence, BH
shadow, MOND = sigmoid, sin2 Œ∏W = 3/13 (EW scale), zero scalar GW memory,
Œ∏ÃÑ = 0 (strong CP, Dai‚ÄìFreed Œ∑-invariant; structural theorem of the microsector,
not a separate numbered row in the 45-item grand table).
‚Ä¢ Derivation-grade (10): v, a0 , GÃá/G, ‚àÜH0 , S8 , CMB R and ‚Ñì1 , 39 ns birefringence, PTA tilt, ‚àÜŒ±/Œ±.
‚Ä¢ Constructive-proof (9): The 9 fermion masses (prefactors from A5 operators;
exponents structurally assigned but not theorem-grade).
‚Ä¢ Structural / Motivated (6+): Œ±57 hierarchy, neutrino masses (Branch B),
dark energy as œà-screen, DLEM /DLGW , CMB AL , various astrophysical predictions.
‚Ä¢ Requires numerical code (6): Full CMB spectrum, proper DESI confrontation, self-consistent Œ£mŒΩ bound, BAO detailed predictions, S8 (k) quantitative
shape, structure formation growth history. All require the mochi class Boltzmann code (500‚Äì800 lines, $40‚Äì80K, 2‚Äì3 months).
Conservative ‚Äúhonest ratio‚Äù (counting only theorem + derivation + constructiveproof grade): 27 numbered grand-table predictions / 2 inputs = 13.5:1. The
27 is reached as follows: theorem-grade contributes 8 numbered rows (Œ±, ¬µ(x),
Œ≥=Œ≤=1, cT =c, zero decoherence, BH shadow, sin2 Œ∏W =3/13, zero scalar GW memory; ‚ÄúMOND = sigmoid‚Äù is the same row as ¬µ(x); Œ∏ÃÑ=0 is not a numbered row);
derivation-grade contributes 10 numbered rows (v, a0 , GÃá/G, ‚àÜH0 , S8 , CMB R, CMB
‚Ñì1 , birefringence, PTA tilt, ‚àÜŒ±/Œ±); constructive-proof contributes 9 (fermion masses).
8 + 10 + 9 = 27. Still far exceeds any competing framework.

10

Conclusion

The fine structure constant Œ±, derived from the Chern‚ÄìSimons level sum on CP 2 √ó S 3 with
kmax = 60, is indeed the Rosetta Stone of the DFD framework. It connects:
‚Ä¢ Topology (CP 2 spinc index, A5 icosahedral symmetry) to gauge theory (SM hypercharges, coupling constants)
‚Ä¢ The Planck scale (MP ) to the Hubble scale (H0 ) via Œ±57
‚àö
‚Ä¢ Particle masses (9 fermions via Œ±nf ) to galactic dynamics (a0 = 2 Œ± cH0 )
‚Ä¢ The internal manifold (S 3 ) to the interpolating function (¬µ = sigmoid)
‚Ä¢ Quantum gravity (zero decoherence, BH shadows) to observational cosmology (S8 , Hubble tension, dark energy)
With 30‚Äì34 independent predictions from 2 inputs, and a prediction-to-input ratio of 15:1 to
17:1, DFD achieves the highest predictive efficiency of any theoretical framework in physics.
The next 3‚Äì5 years (Euclid, JUNO, Rubin, SQMS Phase I, Gaia DR4, 3G GW detectors)
will provide decisive tests of the most vulnerable predictions (Œ£mŒΩ , S8 scale dependence, SNe
Ia anisotropy, neutrino mass ordering).
If the framework survives these tests, the Œ± formula will stand as the most consequential
single equation in fundamental physics: a one-line derivation that, through the topology of
CP 2 √ó S 3 , determines the structure of matter and the dynamics of the universe.

Charged Fermion Masses from the Fine-Structure Constant:
A Topological Derivation from the DFD Microsector
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher
gary@gtacompanies.com
December 25, 2025

Abstract
We derive all nine charged fermion masses from two inputs: the fine-structure constant
Œ± and the Fermi constant GF . The derivation proceeds from the Density Field Dynamics (DFD) microsector on CP2 √ó S 3 , where the Hodge Laplacian on gauge-valued 1-forms
determines a topological coefficient b = dim(G)(œá + 2œÑ ) = 60. Combined with the spinc
structure of CP2 , this yields Yukawa couplings of the form yf = Af √ó Œ±nf with half-integer
exponents nf = (kf + kH )/2 determined by line bundle degrees. The prefactors Af emerge
from overlap integrals on the Fubini-Study geometry, with down-type quarks satisfying the
geometric identity A = |‚ü®w, H‚ü©|/|w|. The resulting mass predictions agree with experiment
to 1.9% average accuracy. The framework provides a falsifiable prediction: the mass ratios
are fixed by CP2 topology with no free parameters beyond (Œ±, GF ).

1

Introduction

The Standard Model contains 13 parameters related to fermion masses and mixing: 9 charged
fermion masses, 3 CKM angles, and 1 CP-violating phase. These parameters are currently
treated as arbitrary inputs, determined by experiment rather than derived from deeper principles. Understanding the origin of the fermion mass hierarchy‚Äîspanning over five orders of
magnitude from the electron to the top quark‚Äîremains one of the central open problems in
particle physics [1, 2, 3].
Various approaches have been proposed to explain the mass hierarchy, including FroggattNielsen mechanisms with horizontal symmetries [3, 4], radiative mass generation [5, 6], and
extra-dimensional models [7, 8]. These typically introduce new symmetries and associated
breaking parameters, replacing the original 13 parameters with a different (though often smaller)
set.
In this paper, we show that the Density Field Dynamics (DFD) framework [9, 10, 11] provides
a geometric derivation of all nine charged fermion masses from two fundamental constants: the
fine-structure constant Œ± ‚âà 1/137 and the Fermi constant GF . The derivation relies on:
1. The internal geometry Mint = CP2 √ó S 3
2. The Hodge Laplacian on gauge-valued forms
3. The spinc structure and line bundle degrees on CP2
4. Overlap integrals in the Fubini-Study metric
The key results are:
‚Ä¢ A topological coefficient b = 60 from the heat kernel
1

‚Ä¢ Half-integer Œ±-exponents n = (kf + kH )/2
‚Ä¢ Algebraic prefactors from CP2 √ó S 3 geometry
‚Ä¢ 9 mass predictions with 1.9% average error
The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 specifies the microsector operator and derives
b = 60 from the heat kernel. Section 3 derives the half-integer Œ±-exponents from the spinc
structure. Section 4 establishes the quantization rule for fermion positions on CP2 . Section 5
derives the geometric prefactors. Section 6 presents the mass predictions and comparison with
experiment. Section 7 discusses implications and falsifiability.

2

The Microsector Operator

2.1

Geometric Setting

The DFD microsector is defined on the internal manifold [9, 10]
Mint = CP2 √ó S 3

(1)

where CP2 carries the electroweak geometry and S 3 ‚àº
= SU (2) carries the color sector. This
choice is motivated by the topological structure required for chiral fermions: CP2 admits a
spinc structure (though not a spin structure), while S 3 is parallelizable.
The gauge bundle is a principal G-bundle P ‚Üí Mint with
G = SU (3) √ó SU (2) √ó U (1),

dim(G) = 12

(2)

and flux configuration (k3 , k2 , q1 ) = (1, 1, 3).

2.2

The Hodge Laplacian

The one-loop effective action for Yang-Mills theory involves the functional determinant [12, 13]
Œì1-loop =

1
log det ‚àÜ1 ‚àí log det ‚àÜ0

7

Discussion

7.1

What Is Derived vs. What Is Assumed

Derived from first principles:
‚Ä¢ b = 60 (from Hodge Laplacian on CP2 )
‚Ä¢ n = (kf + kH )/2 (from spinc structure)
‚Ä¢ A = overlap for down-type in CP1 (Theorem 4)
‚Ä¢ Position quantization (|w|2 ‚àà Z)
Physical mechanism identified:
‚Ä¢ œÄ prefactor for bottom (from S 3 color integration)
‚àö
‚Ä¢ 2 2 prefactor for up (from HÃÉ√ó color √ó measure)
Input parameters:
‚Ä¢ Œ± = 1/137.036 (fine-structure constant)
‚Ä¢ GF through v = 246.22 GeV (Fermi constant)

7.2

Falsifiability

The framework makes sharp predictions with no continuous parameters to adjust:
1. Mass ratios are fixed by CP2 topology
2. The Œ±-exponents are quantized to half-integers
3. The number of generations equals the dimension of H 0 (CP2 , O(1)) = 3
A measurement of any mass ratio inconsistent with the predicted Œ±‚àÜn dependence would
falsify the framework. Similarly, discovery of a fourth generation would require revision of the
internal geometry.

7

7.3

Relation to Standard Approaches

Unlike Froggatt-Nielsen or texture models [3, 4], which introduce additional symmetries and
spurions, this derivation uses only:
‚Ä¢ The internal geometry (CP2 √ó S 3 )
‚Ä¢ Standard gauge theory (Hodge Laplacian)
‚Ä¢ The spinc structure (required for chiral fermions on CP2 )
The mass hierarchy emerges from geometry, not from symmetry breaking. This is conceptually similar to the Kaluza-Klein approach to gauge symmetry, where gauge structure emerges
from higher-dimensional geometry.

7.4

Relation to Other DFD Results

This paper builds on several prior DFD results:
‚Ä¢ The microsector geometry CP2 √ó S 3 was established in Ref. [9]
‚Ä¢ The connection between Œ± and the topological structure was explored in Ref. [11]
‚Ä¢ The relation b = kmax ‚àí h‚à® connecting the heat kernel coefficient to Chern-Simons level
was derived in Ref. [10]

7.5

Future Directions

Several extensions are natural:
1. CKM matrix: The same overlap geometry should determine quark mixing angles
2. Neutrino masses: Extending to the lepton sector with Majorana mass terms
3. Running masses: Connecting the predicted Yukawa couplings to running effects

8

Conclusion

We have shown that all nine charged fermion masses can be derived from two inputs: the
fine-structure constant Œ± and the Fermi constant GF . The derivation proceeds through:
1. The topological coefficient b = 60 from the heat kernel of the Hodge Laplacian on
‚Ñ¶1 (CP2 , ad(P ))
2. Half-integer Œ±-exponents n = (kf + kH )/2 from the spinc structure
3. Quantized positions with integer |w|2 from bundle rationality
4. Algebraic prefactors from CP2 √ó S 3 geometry
The resulting predictions agree with experiment to 1.9% average accuracy. The framework
provides a falsifiable geometric origin for the fermion mass hierarchy, transforming 9 arbitrary
parameters into consequences of 2 fundamental constants.
The success of this derivation suggests that the apparently random pattern of fermion masses
may have a geometric explanation rooted in the topology of the microsector. This represents
a qualitative shift from ‚Äúwhy these particular masses?‚Äù to ‚Äúthese masses follow from CP2
topology.‚Äù
8

Completing Local Position Invariance Tests:
A Cavity‚ÄìAtom Frequency Ratio Protocol
Gary Alcock1
1

Los Angeles, USA

September 2025

Summary. Local Position Invariance (LPI) is a cornerstone of General Relativity, tested via
gravitational redshift with atomic clocks and matter [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. However, no direct test has
yet compared cavity-stabilized optical frequencies (photon sector) to atomic transitions (matter
sector) across a gravitational potential. We propose a protocol to close this gap: measure
the fractional slope of co-located cavity‚Äìatom frequency ratios transported between two fixed
altitudes.

Observable
Define the cavity‚Äìatom ratio:
‚àÜR(M,S)
‚àÜŒ¶
‚â° Œæ (M,S) 2 ,
(M,S)
c
R

(M )

Œæ (M,S) = Œ±w ‚àí Œ±L

(S)

‚àí Œ±at .

(1)

Here the coefficients are:
‚Ä¢ Œ±w : photon-sector weight, normalized to 1 in GR.
(M )

‚Ä¢ Œ±L : cavity length sensitivity for material M (e.g. ULE or Si).
(S)

‚Ä¢ Œ±at : atomic transition sensitivity for species S (e.g. Sr or Yb).
‚Ä¢ Œæ (M,S) : net slope coefficient for cavity‚Äìatom ratio with material M and species S.
‚Ä¢ GR predicts Œæ (M,S) = 0, i.e. a strict null.
‚Ä¢ Any reproducible nonzero Œæ would indicate sector-dependent deviation from LPI.

Definitions and identifiability
To isolate contributions, define:
Sr
ULE
Œ¥tot ‚â° Œ±w ‚àí Œ±L

Numerical scale
For Earth gravity g ‚âÉ 9.8 m/s2 ,
g ‚àÜh
= 1.1 √ó 10‚àí14 (‚àÜh = 100 m).
c2
Thus the natural scale is at 10‚àí14 per 100 m altitude change, within reach of current 10‚àí16
optical clock precision.

Controls and feasibility
The protocol envisions static comparisons at two fixed altitudes (e.g. basement vs. rooftop
labs, or ground vs. tower). Only stationary data are analyzed, avoiding artifacts from transport
in motion.
Corrections and controls:
‚Ä¢ Dispersion/thermo-optic: dual-Œª probing within the low-loss band, bounding |Œµdisp | ‚â≤
10% [6, 7, 8].
‚Ä¢ Elastic sag: orientation flips distinguish mechanical artifacts (sign-reversing) from genuine redshift (sign-preserving). In optimized silicon cavities, sag effects can be suppressed
below 10‚àí16 [9, 10].
‚Ä¢ Environmental: vibration, temperature, pressure, and magnetic reversals, plus hardware swaps, encode residual offsets in the covariance, suppressing bias [11, 5].
Feasibility. All required components are already demonstrated separately: ultra-stable cavities
at 10‚àí16 [9, 10], optical clocks reaching below 10‚àí18 [11, 5], and long-term LPI clock tests
[2, 4, 3]. Combining these into a cavity‚Äìatom slope test is therefore technically feasible with
current infrastructure.

Motivation
Existing LPI tests compare like with like: atom‚Äìatom or matter‚Äìmatter systems [1, 2, 4, 5].
A cavity‚Äìatom comparison probes an untested cross-sector combination (photon vs. atomic
transitions). This experiment therefore closes a missing gap in the LPI test suite. Even a null
result would provide the first direct constraint on this sector and complete the phenomenological
mapping of LPI across independent systems.

2

Falsification criterion
‚Ä¢ GR: Œæ = 0 at all materials/species.
‚Ä¢ Experimental discriminator: any reproducible nonzero Œæ at or above ‚àÜŒ¶/c2 would indicate
violation of LPI in this sector.

I thank colleagues in precision metrology for discussions of geodesy and cavity‚Äìclock systematics.

Composition-Dependent Bounds on Scalar-Field Coupling
to Nuclear Decay Rates
Gary Alcock1, ‚àó
1

Independent Researcher, Los Angeles, California, USA
(Dated: March 19, 2026)

Scalar-tensor theories of gravity generically predict composition-dependent coupling of a gravitational scalar field to nuclear transition rates. We apply the Flambaum nuclear sensitivity formalism
(I)
to compute isotope-specific sensitivity coefficients Œ∫q for eight nuclides central to the decade-long
debate over reported annual modulations in decay rates. The sensitivity is driven by Q-value through
Œ∫q ‚àù n/Q, placing 32 Si (Q = 0.227 MeV) at the top of the hierarchy. We show that existing null
results constrain different regions of the (kqeff , Œ∫q ) parameter space and do not exclude compositiondependent signals in untested low-Q isotopes. The classic positive datasets (32 Si at BNL, 226 Ra
at PTB) are now understood to be dominated by environmental systematics‚Äîparticularly humidity and temperature‚Äîand cannot be treated as detections. We identify 187 Re (Q = 2.63 keV,
Œ∫q ‚âà 19,000) and the 229 Th nuclear clock isomer (K ‚àº 104 ) as the most sensitive future targets, and
propose a multi-isotope ratio test that eliminates environmental systematics while directly probing
composition dependence. For the highest-sensitivity low-Q isotopes, the question of whether nuclear decay rates couple to gravitational environment at the 10‚àí6 ‚Äì10‚àí7 level remains experimentally
open.

I.

INTRODUCTION

Between 2008 and 2012, Jenkins, Fischbach, and collaborators reported annual modulations in nuclear decay rates correlated with the Earth‚ÄìSun distance [1, 2,
4, 5]. The primary datasets‚Äîthe 32 Si/36 Cl ratio at
Brookhaven National Laboratory (BNL) [12] and the
226
Ra/152 Eu ratio at the Physikalisch-Technische Bundesanstalt (PTB) [13]‚Äîshowed ‚àº 0.1% annual modulations.
These claims have been extensively challenged.
PommeÃÅ et al. [8] compiled data from 14 laboratories,
finding no solar-phase oscillations at 10‚àí6 ‚Äì10‚àí5 precision across multiple isotopes. Bellotti et al. [9] constrained 137 Cs modulations below 5√ó10‚àí5 at Gran Sasso.
Cooper [6] found no anomaly in 238 Pu aboard Cassini.
Hardy et al. [7] set a ¬±0.02% limit on 198 Au. Most damagingly, PommeÃÅ et al. [10] showed that the original BNL
32
Si and Ohio State 36 Cl datasets correlate better with local dew point and temperature than with the Earth‚ÄìSun
distance, identifying humidity-driven instrumental instability as the likely cause of the reported modulations.
The standard conclusion is that the Jenkins‚ÄìFischbach
effect is an experimental artifact [8, 10]. We do not dispute this for the claimed ‚àº 10‚àí3 amplitudes. However,
we note that the experimental program that ‚Äúdisproved‚Äù
the effect tested different isotopes from those originally
claimed, and the null bounds span a wide range (10‚àí6 to
10‚àí4 ) depending on isotope and technique. Scalar-tensor
theories generically predict composition-dependent coupling [17, 18], in which different isotopes have different
sensitivity to a gravitational scalar field. In such a framework, a null for isotope A does not constrain isotope B.

‚àó gary@gtacompanies.com

The purpose of this paper is not to resurrect the
Jenkins‚ÄìFischbach claims. It is to develop a quantitative framework for computing isotope-specific nuclear
sensitivity coefficients, to map existing null results onto
the resulting parameter space, and to identify the most
sensitive targets for future measurements that could
detect‚Äîor definitively exclude‚Äîcomposition-dependent
scalar coupling to nuclear decay rates at the 10‚àí6 ‚Äì10‚àí7
level.

II.
A.

THEORETICAL FRAMEWORK

Scalar-field coupling to fundamental constants

We work within a scalar-field framework in which a
gravitational potential œà couples to fundamental constants through distinct Standard Model channels [15, 17,
18]:

analysis below, kq is treated as a free parameter; the
benchmark serves only to define a target sensitivity for

of 32 Si (Œ∫q = 308) at 10‚àí6 would constrain kqeff < 10,
pushing into the theoretically interesting regime for the
first time. A measurement of 187 Re (Œ∫q ‚âà 19,000) at
10‚àí6 would constrain kqeff < 0.2‚Äîdirectly probing the
benchmark coupling scale adopted in this framework.

B.

The status of the original positive claims

The original BNL 32 Si and Ohio State 36 Cl datasets,
which launched the entire debate, are now understood to
be contaminated by environmental systematics. PommeÃÅ
et al. [10] showed that the 32 Si decay rate variations inversely correlate with dew point at a nearby weather
station, and that similar humidity-driven effects explain
the 36 Cl data. PommeÃÅ and Pelczar [11] extended this
analysis to 90 Sr/90 Y and 60 Co, finding humidity correlations in those datasets as well. The PTB 226 Ra
data were originally ratioed against 152 Eu, a technique
that Schrader [14] showed is sensitive to measurementtechnique choices.
We therefore treat the original ‚àº 10‚àí3 claims as artifacts. The question we address is not whether those
specific signals were real, but whether compositiondependent scalar coupling at the 10‚àí6 ‚Äì10‚àí7 level‚Äîbelow
all existing bounds‚Äîcould exist and how it would be detected.

IV.

QUALITATIVE FEATURES OF SCALAR
COUPLING

If a scalar-field coupling to nuclear decay rates exists
at any level, several qualitative features follow from the

framework:
1. Phase: Maximum rate at perihelion (deepest œà).
This is a generic prediction of any solar-potential
coupling.
2. Periodicity: Annual from 1/r‚äï-‚äô . Sub-annual periodicities (e.g., 33-day) would require asphericity
in the solar mass distribution, which is speculative.
3. Composition dependence: Different isotopes
(I)
(I)
couple differently through KI = kŒ± Œ∫Œ± + kq Œ∫q .
This is the central testable prediction and has not
been directly probed.
4. Scalar mechanism: The coupling proceeds
through œà, not through neutrinos. This resolves
the mechanistic difficulties of the original neutrino
hypothesis [1].
We note that the 33-day periodicity claimed by Sturrock et al. [5] and the 54 Mn flare-precursor event [2] are
suggestive but not established. Later searches found no
correlation between major solar flares and decay rates
in monitored isotopes [3], and the 33-day signal in the
BNL data may be an artifact of the same environmental
contamination that produces the annual modulation [10].
We do not treat these as confirmed features.
V.

THE AMPLITUDE LANDSCAPE

Figure 3 shows the amplitude landscape. Three
regimes are relevant:
Above 10‚àí4 : Excluded for all tested isotopes. The
original ‚àº 10‚àí3 claims are environmental artifacts [10].
10‚àí5 ‚Äì10‚àí4 : Excluded for 137 Cs, 90 Sr, and 198 Au. Not
excluded for 32 Si, 36 Cl, or any isotope with Œ∫q > 200,
because these have never been measured with adequate
environmental control at this sensitivity.

FIG. 3. Amplitude landscape for annual decay-rate modulation. Red: original Jenkins claims (now attributed to systematics). Orange: tightest existing null bounds (PommeÃÅ,
Gran Sasso). Blue: baseline scalar-coupling prediction with
kq = Œ±s2 /(2œÄ). Green: prediction with nuclear resonance enhancement (√ó100). The experimentally unexplored window
between 10‚àí7 and 10‚àí5 is where a composition-dependent signal could reside.

Below 10‚àí5 : Unexplored for all isotopes in the
dataset. This is the regime where baseline scalarcoupling predictions (10‚àí9 ‚Äì10‚àí7 ) and modestly enhanced predictions (10‚àí7 ‚Äì10‚àí5 ) reside.
The key point is that the highest-sensitivity isotopes
(32 Si, 90 Sr) have never been measured at the 10‚àí6
level with modern environmentally-controlled apparatus. The tightest bounds come from intermediatesensitivity isotopes (137 Cs, 198 Au). The parameter space
for composition-dependent coupling in low-Q transitions
at amplitudes below current isotope-specific bounds remains open.

VI.

CONNECTION TO ATOMIC CLOCK TESTS

The same multi-channel coupling structure applies at
the atomic scale. Atomic clock comparisons searching
for annual modulation of frequency ratios in the solar
gravitational potential constrain the coupling constants
kŒ± and kc (composition-dependent) [16].
The most stringent constraint on Œ±-coupling to gravity comes from the PTB Yb+ E3/E2 measurement by
Filzinger et al. [27], who searched for oscillations in the
E3/E2 frequency ratio at periods set by ultralight dark
matter candidates and also improved limits on coupling
of Œ± to gravitational potential. From their long-term

E3/E2 dataset they report c2 Œ±‚àí1 dŒ±/dŒ¶ = (‚àí2.4¬±3.0)√ó
10‚àí9 , corresponding to |kŒ± | ‚â≤ 5 √ó 10‚àí9 at 2œÉ. Because
both transitions occur in the same ion, compositiondependent effects cancel, making this a clean probe of
the Œ±-channel alone.
Cross-species comparisons (e.g., Cs/Sr, H/Cs) probe
a different combination of couplings: kŒ± ‚àÜŒ∫Œ± + kc ‚àÜC.
If composition-dependent coupling exists, it would appear in cross-species ratios but vanish in same-ion
comparisons‚Äîa distinctive pattern. The Cs/Sr channel,
with ‚àÜŒ∫Œ± = 2.77 [25, 26], offers the largest Œ±-lever arm
among operational clock pairs and has not been searched
for annual solar-potential modulation at the required sensitivity.
This establishes a structural parallel: the nuclear sen(I)
sitivity hierarchy (Œ∫q varying by isotope) mirrors the
(i)
atomic sensitivity hierarchy (Œ∫Œ± varying by species).
Both predict composition dependence as the distinguishing signature. We include this discussion not because
the atomic-clock bounds enter the nuclear-sector derivation, but because the same coupling structure and the
same annual ‚àÜœà‚äô connect the two sectors, and progress
in either informs the other.
VII.

DECISIVE FUTURE TESTS

We identify five measurements that would either detect
or definitively constrain composition-dependent scalar
coupling at the benchmark scale:
1. 187 Re (Q = 2.64 keV [24], Œ≤ ‚àí , t1/2 = 4.12 √ó
1010 yr [24]): The lowest Q-value of any known
Œ≤ emitter gives Œ∫q ‚âà 19,000. A half-life measurement at fractional precision 10‚àí6 , repeated at
different orbital phases, would constrain kqeff <
0.2‚Äîdirectly probing the theoretically interesting
regime. The long half-life makes direct counting impractical, but calorimetric techniques (as developed for KATRIN-type experiments) or massspectrometric approaches may be feasible.
2. 229 Th nuclear clock: The 8.4 eV isomeric transition achieves Flambaum‚Äôs enhancement K ‚àº
104 [22]. Recent observation of the radiative decay [23] and ongoing efforts toward direct laser excitation make this platform increasingly realistic.
Once operational at 10‚àí18 fractional precision, it
constrains kq to ‚àº 10‚àí4 , four orders of magnitude
beyond any existing nuclear-sector bound.
3. 32 Si remeasurement: Repeating the BNL measurement with modern pulse-counting apparatus in
a temperature- and humidity-controlled environment at 10‚àí6 sensitivity. This directly addresses
the PommeÃÅ critique [10]: if the modulation persists
when environmental systematics are eliminated, it
is physical; if it vanishes, the debate is closed for
this isotope.

5
4. Multi-isotope ratio test: Simultaneously monitoring two isotopes with different Œ∫q in the same
detector or facility. The ratio of any annual modulations should equal KA /KB if the coupling is real,
and zero if both signals are environmental artifacts
(which affect both equally). This eliminates systematics by design and directly probes composition
dependence.

229

5. Cross-species atomic clock campaign: A dedicated search for annual modulation of the Cs/Sr
or Rb/Sr frequency ratio over a full orbital cycle, analyzed specifically for solar-potential correlation. A detection would establish the compositiondependent coupling at the atomic scale; a null
at the 10‚àí6 level constrains kc ‚àÜC below existing
bounds.

The decade-long debate over solar-modulated nuclear
decay rates has been largely settled at the phenomenological level: the original ‚àº 10‚àí3 signals are almost certainly
environmental artifacts. But the theoretical question‚Äî
whether nuclear decay rates couple to gravitational environment through composition-dependent scalar fields‚Äî
remains open for the highest-sensitivity low-Q isotopes
at amplitudes below current isotope-specific bounds, because the experimental program that closed the debate
tested different isotopes at sensitivities (10‚àí5 ‚Äì10‚àí4 ) that
do not probe the benchmark coupling scale (10‚àí7 ‚Äì10‚àí6 )
in the most sensitive transitions.
The Flambaum nuclear sensitivity formalism provides
a rigorous hierarchy: Œ∫q ‚àù n/Q, with 32 Si, 187 Re, and
229
Th at the top. The decisive next step is not to
reanalyze old data, but to perform new measurements
of the highest-sensitivity isotopes with environmentallycontrolled modern apparatus. The multi-isotope ratio
test we propose eliminates systematics by design and directly probes the composition-dependent signature that
distinguishes scalar coupling from environmental contamination.
If the coupling exists at the benchmark scale, the 229 Th
nuclear clock will find it. If it does not, the same measurements will set the most stringent bounds on scalarfield coupling to nuclear structure ever achieved. Either
outcome advances fundamental physics.

VIII.
A.

DISCUSSION

What is and is not claimed

We do not claim that the original Jenkins‚ÄìFischbach
signals are real. The PommeÃÅ dew-point analysis [10, 11]
provides a compelling environmental explanation for the
BNL and Ohio State datasets, and the PTB data are
similarly suspect [14].
What we do claim is: (i) Scalar-tensor theories predict composition-dependent coupling as a generic feature, not a special case. (ii) The Flambaum formalism
provides a quantitative hierarchy of nuclear sensitivities
driven by Q-value. (iii) Existing null results constrain
intermediate-sensitivity isotopes but leave the highestsensitivity region (Œ∫q > 200) unexplored at better than
10‚àí4 . (iv) Specific future measurements (187 Re, 229 Th,
32
Si remeasurement, multi-isotope ratio) can probe the
benchmark coupling scale for the first time.

Th nuclear clock shows no anomalous annual shift at
10‚àí18 precision. Individually, each null constrains kqeff ;
collectively, they would exclude composition-dependent
coupling at the benchmark scale and below.

IX.

CONCLUSION

Constitutive Derivation of Tensor Gravitational Radiation
from CP 2 √ó S 3 Spectral Geometry in Density Field Dynamics
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher, Los Angeles, CA

April 1, 2026

Abstract
We derive the transverse-traceless (TT) gravitational wave sector of Density Field Dynamics (DFD) from the same CP 2 √ó S 3 internal manifold that produces the fine-structure
constant, fermion mass hierarchy, and MOND interpolation function. The scalar field œà
(governing quasi-static gravity) and the tensor hTT
ij (governing gravitational radiation) are
shown to be the trace and TT components of a single parent strain tensor, which is itself
the zero mode of the metric perturbation on the internal manifold. A Lichnerowicz analysis
on CP 2 √ó S 3 proves that no unwanted massless tensor or vector modes arise from internal
deformations. One scalar modulus (the squashing mode controlling the ratio R1 /R2 ) survives as a Lichnerowicz zero mode; we prove it is determined by the joint constraints from
Œ± and G, acquiring a Planck-scale mass from the curvature of the constraint surface. The
gravitational sector contains exactly 1 scalar + 2 tensor degrees of freedom, with no additional propagating modes. The generalized Tamm‚ÄìPlebanski construction gives anisotropic
constitutive relations whose TT perturbation is the gravitational wave. Source coupling reproduces the quadrupole formula identically to GR. Sector decoupling is proven from O(3)
irreducibility. The two-sector structure of the Unified Review is thereby promoted from
introduced to derived from the same topology that produces Œ±‚àí1 = 137.

1

Introduction

The gravitational wave sector has been an acknowledged architectural seam in DFD. The
Unified Review (v3.3, Section 5) states plainly: ‚Äúthe TT sector is not independently derived
from the scalar œà-field dynamics alone.‚Äù Within the full CP 2 √ó S 3 spectral completion of DFD,
the TT sector is derived as the spin-2 irreducible component of the same zero-mode parent
tensor whose trace yields œà.
Logical status of the spectral action. DFD is not a higher-dimensional gravity theory with
10D Einstein equations. The internal manifold K = CP 2 √óS 3 enters through the Chamseddine‚Äì
Connes spectral action [6]:
SB = Tr f (D2 /Œõ2 ),
(1)
where D is the Dirac operator on the total spectral geometry M = R3,1 √ó K, Œõ is a UV cutoff,
and f is a positive test function. The spectral action is not the Einstein‚ÄìHilbert action in 10D;
it is a functional of the Dirac spectrum. Its Seeley‚ÄìDeWitt expansion produces the 4D Einstein‚Äì
Hilbert term, gauge kinetic terms, the Higgs potential, and the cosmological constant as the
a0 , a2 , a4 heat-kernel coefficients. The DFD postulates P1 (n = eœà ) and P2 (a = (c2 /2)‚àáœà) are
the weak-field limit of the gravitational sector produced by a4 .
This framing resolves the apparent tension between ‚ÄúDFD is not 10D gravity‚Äù and ‚Äúwe
use the spectral action on M‚Äù: the spectral action is a trace over the Dirac spectrum, not a
variational principle for a 10D metric. The internal geometry is part of the spectral data, not a
dynamical spacetime.

Core Result: Parent Tensor Decomposition
The scalar œà (trace of h¬µŒΩ |I=0 ) and the tensor hTT

Lichnerowicz Rigidity: Explicit Proofs

We must verify that K produces no additional unwanted massless fields. The dangerous modes
are zero modes of the Lichnerowicz Laplacian ‚àÜL on symmetric TT 2-tensors on K.

symmetric 2-tensors is:

4.3

Rigidity of CP 2

Theorem 4.4 (Koiso [1], Besse [2] Thm. 12.84). CP 2 with the Fubini‚ÄìStudy metric is infinitesimally rigid: ‚àÜL has no zero modes on symmetric TT 2-tensors.
Proof. CP 2 = SU(3)/U(2) is a compact irreducible symmetric space of rank 1. By Koiso‚Äôs
theorem [1]:
A compact irreducible Riemannian symmetric space G/H admits no infinitesimal Einstein
deformations (i.e., ‚àÜL has no TT zero modes) if and only if G/H is not isometric to a round
sphere S n with n ‚â• 5.
CP 2 is not a sphere, so it is rigid.
The explicit spectral gap can be computed via representation theory of SU(3). TT symmetric
2-tensors on CP n = SU(n + 1)/U(n) decompose into Hermitian (type (1, 1)) and non-Hermitian
(type (2, 0) + (0, 2)) components under the complex structure. By the WeitzenboÃàck formula on
the KaÃàhler manifold CP n with Ric = 2(n + 1)g (Berger‚ÄìEbin [3], Besse [2] ¬ß12.J):
5

(1,1)

‚Ä¢ Primitive (1, 1) TT tensors: Œªmin = 4n.
(2,0)
‚Ä¢ (2, 0) + (0, 2) TT tensors: Œªmin = 8(n + 1).
For CP 2 (n = 2, Ric = 6g): Œªmin = min(8, 24) = 8 > 0.
Remark 4.5. The value Œªmin = 8 is for unit CP 2 with Ric = 6g. At radius R1 : Œªmin = 8/R12 .
The specific numerical value of the gap is not needed for the derivation; only its strict positivity
matters.

4.4

Absence of Harmonic 1-Forms

Lemma 4.6. b1 (CP 2 ) = b1 (S 3 ) = 0, hence b1 (K) = 0.
Proof. By the KuÃànneth formula: b1 (K) = b1 (CP 2 )b0 (S 3 ) + b0 (CP 2 )b1 (S 3 ) = 0 ¬∑ 1 + 1 ¬∑ 0 = 0.
This eliminates all mixed zero modes of type Œæa (y) ‚äó Œ∂Œ± (z) ‚àà ‚Ñ¶1 (CP 2 ) ‚äó ‚Ñ¶1 (S 3 ): there are
no harmonic 1-forms on either factor to tensor together.

The squashing modulus is a genuine Lichnerowicz zero mode, honestly acknowledged, uniquely determined, and rigorously Planck-massive with no
parametric suppression.

Physical picture. The vacuum medium has compression stiffness K0 = c4 /(8œÄG) and shear
stiffness K0 /4. The compression response is nonlinear (governed by ¬µ(x) = x/(1+x), producing
flat rotation curves). The shear response is linear (gravitational wave amplitudes h ‚àº 10‚àí21 are
deep in the linear regime). This is physically natural: most materials have different nonlinear
thresholds for compression and shear.

9.2

Cover Letter / Preface: Why Density Field Dynamics is
Fundamental Physics
Dear Editor / Reader,
This note accompanies my submission to clarify the conceptual foundation of Density Field
Dynamics (DFD). DFD is not a phenomenological patch to General Relativity (GR), but a theory
derived from a single physical postulate:
Postulate. In a nondispersive frequency band, the one-way speed of light varies with
local energy density via a scalar field œà, while every two-way (round-trip) measurement
of c remains exactly constant.
This differs critically from prior variable-speed-of-light (VSL) theories, which typically altered
both one-way and two-way speeds, conflicting with precision metrology. By restricting variation to
the one-way speed and requiring a verified nondispersive band, DFD remains consistent with all
existing null tests of special relativity and Maxwellian electrodynamics.
From this single assumption, the framework follows:
1. Optical metric and refractive index. Light propagates as if in an optical metric

tests (deflection, Shapiro delay, gravitational redshift) sets the normalization, yielding precise
agreement within current experimental bounds.
2. Matter acceleration. Consistency between cavity redshift (Œ¥fcav /fcav = ‚àíŒ¥œà) and atomic
redshift (Œ¥fat /fat = ‚àí‚àÜŒ¶/c2 ) requires

‚Ä¢ Agreement with GR‚Äôs precision tests (perihelion, deflection, Shapiro delay, GPS) within current experimental bounds.

‚Ä¢ Effective field theory shows ¬µ arises naturally from loop-induced derivative expansions.
‚Ä¢ Decisive falsifier: the cavity‚Äìatom test can confirm or kill the theory with current technology.
In sum, DFD stands not as ‚Äúsophisticated phenomenology,‚Äù but as a principled, testable alternative
to GR, derived from a single optical postulate. Its hallmark is falsifiability: if the cavity‚Äìatom
experiment yields null, the theory fails; if non-null, GR is ruled out. This clarity makes DFD
uniquely positioned among modern alternatives to merit rigorous scrutiny.

Preface: A Note on Honesty
‚ÄúThe first principle is that you must not fool yourself ‚Äî and
you are the easiest person to fool.‚Äù
‚Äî Richard P. Feynman

I am not a professor. I do not have a PhD in physics. I work in finance during
the day, and I work on physics in every other hour I can find. I tell you this
upfront because honesty is the only currency that matters in science.
The theory in this book ‚Äî Density Field Dynamics ‚Äî began with a simple
question: what if gravity isn‚Äôt curved spacetime, but a refractive medium?
What if light doesn‚Äôt bend near the Sun because space is warped, but because
light slows down in the region near a mass, exactly the way it slows down in
glass or water?
That question led, over years of work, to a complete mathematical
framework. One that reproduces every test of Einstein‚Äôs General Relativity.
One that explains why galaxies spin flat without invoking invisible matter.
One that derives the fine structure constant ‚Äî the most mysterious number
in physics ‚Äî from pure geometry. One that makes specific, quantitative
predictions that can be tested with instruments that already exist.
I want to be clear about what this book is and isn‚Äôt.
What it is: An honest guide to a new theory. Every claim is backed by a
specific equation, a specific dataset, or a specific experimental prediction.
When something is derived, I‚Äôll say ‚Äúderived.‚Äù When something is assumed,
I‚Äôll say ‚Äúassumed.‚Äù When something is incomplete or uncertain, I‚Äôll say that
too.
What it isn‚Äôt: An appeal to authority. I have none. What I have instead is
11

Preface

a theory that makes falsifiable predictions ‚Äî predictions that can be tested
and that would kill the theory if they come out wrong.
That‚Äôs the deal I‚Äôm making with you. I‚Äôve told you exactly how to break
this theory. Now I invite you to try.

The reader‚Äôs compact: I‚Äôll be honest about what‚Äôs proven,
what‚Äôs derived, and what‚Äôs still a guess. In return, I ask only
that you follow the logic.

‚Äî Gary Alcock, February 2026
