4

The Bridge Lemma

Lemma 1 (Bridge Lemma). The topological coefficient b from the CP2 heat kernel equals the
bare CS level corresponding to the UV cutoff:
b = kmax ‚àí h‚à®

(12)

Proof. The quantum shift in SU(2) Chern-Simons theory replaces the bare level k with the
effective level keff = k + h‚à® = k + 2 in all physical quantities.
The UV cutoff kmax = 62 is the effective level at which the sum is truncated. The corresponding bare level is:
kbare = kmax ‚àí h‚à® = 62 ‚àí 2 = 60
(13)
The heat kernel coefficient b = 60 counts the bare degrees of freedom in the gauge sector,
before the quantum shift is applied. This is because the heat kernel expansion is a semiclassical
(one-loop) calculation that does not include the non-perturbative quantum shift.
Therefore b = kbare = kmax ‚àí h‚à® .

4.1

Physical Interpretation

The bridge lemma has a clear physical interpretation:
1. The heat kernel counts semiclassical degrees of freedom. It sees the ‚Äúbare‚Äù gauge structure with b = 60 effective modes.
2. The CS partition function includes the full quantum theory. The quantum shift k ‚Üí
k + h‚à® promotes the bare count to the effective count: 60 ‚Üí 62.
3. The lattice simulations discover that kmax = 62 is the physical cutoff. This is the
quantum value, including the shift.
4. The fermion masses depend on the bare value b = 60, because the Yukawa couplings
are computed from semiclassical overlap integrals on CP2 .
The bridge lemma thus explains why two independent calculations‚Äîone quantum (lattice
CS), one semiclassical (heat kernel)‚Äîyield results differing by exactly h‚à® = 2.
3

5

Consistency Checks

5.1

Check 1: The Quantum Shift is Universal

The value h‚à® = 2 is not adjustable‚Äîit is fixed by the Lie algebra of SU(2). Any other shift
would be inconsistent with:
‚Ä¢ The modular properties of the WZW model
‚Ä¢ The framing dependence of the CS partition function
‚Ä¢ The representation theory of affine SU(2)

5.2

Check 2: Both Calculations Are Independent

The two derivations use completely different mathematics:
‚Ä¢ kmax : Lattice Monte Carlo + CS partition function + Wilson loop observables
‚Ä¢ b: Index theorem + Seeley-DeWitt expansion + CP2 topology
That they agree (up to the quantum shift) is a non-trivial consistency check.

5.3

Check 3: The Dimension Formula

The heat kernel formula b = dim(G)(œá + 2œÑ ) can be rewritten as:
b = 12 √ó 5 = 60

(14)

kmax = b + h‚à® = 60 + 2 = 62

(15)

The CS truncation gives:

If we had used a different gauge group or internal manifold, both b and kmax would change,
but the relation kmax = b + h‚à® would remain valid (with the appropriate h‚à® ).

6

Implications

6.1

Unification of the Two Programs

The bridge lemma unifies the Œ±-derivation program and the fermion mass program:

Both programs access the same underlying microsector structure, but from different limits:
‚Ä¢ The Œ± program works in the full quantum theory
‚Ä¢ The mass program works in the one-loop approximation

4

6.2

Predictive Power

The bridge lemma has predictive power for other gauge groups. If the microsector were based
on SU(3) instead of SU(2), we would predict:
SU(3)
kmax
= b + h‚à®
SU(3) = 60 + 3 = 63

(16)

This could in principle be tested by lattice simulations of SU(3) Chern-Simons theory.

6.3

Why SU(2)?

The microsector uses SU(2) rather than SU(3) because:
1. S 3 ‚àº
= SU(2) is the natural fiber for the color sector
2. The WZW/CS correspondence is cleanest for SU(2)
3. The quantum shift h‚à® = 2 gives the correct relation to b = 60

7

Conclusion

We have proven the bridge lemma connecting the UV cutoff kmax = 62 from lattice ChernSimons simulations to the topological coefficient b = 60 from the heat kernel on CP2 :
b = kmax ‚àí h‚à® = 62 ‚àí 2 = 60

(17)

This result has three important consequences:
1. Consistency: Two independent calculations agree up to the quantum shift, providing a
non-trivial check of the DFD microsector framework.
2. Unification: The Œ±-derivation and fermion mass programs are revealed as quantum and
semiclassical limits of the same underlying structure.
3. Prediction: The bridge lemma can be tested for other gauge groups, providing further
falsifiable predictions.
The bridge lemma closes the theoretical loop between the fine-structure constant and the
fermion mass hierarchy, showing that both emerge from the same CP2 √óS 3 microsector geometry.

The DFD Standard Model:
A Geometric Origin for Œ±, Fermion Masses, and Quark Mixing
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher
gary@gtacompanies.com
December 25, 2025

Abstract
We present a unified account of how the Standard Model parameters emerge from the
Density Field Dynamics (DFD) microsector geometry CP2 √ó S 3 . Three independent derivations are shown to be interconnected: (1) the fine-structure constant Œ± = 1/137 from UVtruncated Chern-Simons theory with kmax = 62; (2) the nine charged fermion masses from
Yukawa couplings yf = Af Œ±nf with topological coefficient b = 60; (3) the CKM quark
mixing matrix from overlap geometry on CP2 . The bridge lemma b = kmax ‚àí h‚à® connects
the Œ±-derivation to the mass derivation via the quantum shift in Chern-Simons theory. Together, these results reduce 14 Standard Model parameters (9 masses, 4 CKM, 1 coupling)
to consequences of two fundamental inputs (Œ±, GF ) and the topology of CP2 √ó S 3 . Average
mass prediction accuracy is 1.9%, and the CKM hierarchy is qualitatively correct. This
represents a significant reduction in the arbitrariness of the Standard Model.

1

Introduction and Overview

The Standard Model of particle physics contains approximately 25 free parameters, including:
‚Ä¢ 3 gauge couplings (g1 , g2 , g3 )
‚Ä¢ 9 charged fermion masses (or equivalently, 9 Yukawa couplings)
‚Ä¢ 3 neutrino masses (or 2 mass differences)
‚Ä¢ 4 CKM parameters (3 angles + 1 phase)
‚Ä¢ 4 PMNS parameters (3 angles + 1 Dirac phase, plus 2 Majorana phases)
‚Ä¢ 2 Higgs parameters (¬µ2 , Œª)
‚Ä¢ 1 QCD Œ∏-parameter
In this paper, we show that within the Density Field Dynamics (DFD) framework, a significant fraction of these parameters‚Äîspecifically, the fine-structure constant, the 9 charged fermion
masses, and the 4 CKM parameters‚Äîcan be derived from a single underlying geometry: the
microsector CP2 √ó S 3 .
The key results, developed in a series of companion papers [2, 3, 4, 5], are:
1. Œ± from Chern-Simons [2]: The fine-structure constant emerges from the vacuum expectation value of the SU(2)k Chern-Simons microsector, with a physical UV cutoff at
kmax = 62.
2. Masses from
topology [3]: The nine charged fermion masses are given by mf =
‚àö
(Af Œ±nf v)/ 2, where the exponent nf = (kf + kH )/2 comes from the spinc structure
of CP2 , and the prefactor Af comes from overlap integrals. Average accuracy: 1.9%.
3. The bridge lemma [4]: The topological coefficient b = 60 from the heat kernel equals
kmax ‚àí h‚à® = 62 ‚àí 2, connecting the Œ±-derivation to the mass derivation.
4. CKM from geometry [5]: The quark mixing matrix arises from the angular configuration of quark positions on CP2 , with the Cabibbo angle related to the 30¬∞ separation
between s and d quarks.

The Microsector Geometry

2.1

The Internal Manifold CP2 √ó S 3

The DFD microsector is defined on the internal manifold
Mint = CP2 √ó S 3

(1)

This choice is motivated by several considerations:
1. CP2 : The complex projective plane is the simplest compact KaÃàhler manifold that admits
a spinc structure (but not a spin structure). Its topology:
œá(CP2 ) = 3,
H 2 (CP2 , Z) = Z

œÑ (CP2 ) = 1

(generated by hyperplane class H)

(3)

2. S 3 : The 3-sphere is isomorphic to SU(2) as a Lie group, making it the natural fiber for
the color sector. It supports Chern-Simons theory at level k.
3. Product structure: The product CP2 √ó S 3 separates electroweak geometry (CP2 ) from
color geometry (S 3 ).

The Standard Model gauge group is embedded via flux quantization on CP2 .

Physical Interpretation

dominated by the quantum-active modes below kmax = 62.

The Bridge Lemma

5.1

Statement

The bridge lemma connects the Œ±-derivation and mass derivation:
b = kmax ‚àí h‚à® = 62 ‚àí 2 = 60

5.2

Physical Interpretation

‚Ä¢ The heat kernel (b = 60) counts semiclassical (bare) degrees of freedom
‚Ä¢ The CS partition function (kmax = 62) includes the quantum shift h‚à® = 2
‚Ä¢ The difference is exactly the dual Coxeter number of SU(2)

2. The quantum shift in CS theory is physically realized
3. Both calculations are consistent (non-trivial check)

‚Ä¢ The Higgs mass (requires full scalar potential analysis)
‚Ä¢ CP-violating phases (qualitative origin identified, quantitative derivation pending)

8.2

3. Lattice verification of kmax = 62 with independent methods
4. CP violation measurements vs. CP2 complex structure predictions

Conclusion

We have presented a unified account of how Standard Model parameters emerge from the DFD
microsector geometry CP2 √ó S 3 :
1. Œ± = 1/137 from UV-truncated Chern-Simons theory
2. 9 fermion masses from yf = Af Œ±nf with 1.9% accuracy
3. b = 60 from the heat kernel, connected to kmax = 62 via the quantum shift
4. CKM hierarchy from Fubini-Study distances on CP2
This reduces 14 flavor parameters to 2 fundamental inputs (Œ±, GF ) plus the topology of
CP2 √ó S 3 .
The success of this framework suggests that the apparently arbitrary parameters of the
Standard Model may have a deep geometric origin. The fermion mass hierarchy, which spans
five orders of magnitude, emerges naturally from integer and half-integer powers of Œ±. The CKM
hierarchy emerges from the angular configuration of quark positions. Both are consequences of
the same underlying geometry.
Future work will extend this framework to neutrino masses, CP-violating phases, and potentially the remaining gauge couplings and Higgs parameters.

The Physical Origin of the Refractive Field in Density Field Dynamics: Gravity as
Electromagnetic Vacuum Loading
G. T. Alcock1
1

Independent Research
(Dated: March 19, 2026)
We develop the interpretation that gravity arises from electromagnetic energy loading of the quantum vacuum, treated as a physical storage medium with vacuum force scale K0 = c4 /(8œÄG). The
dimensionless scalar field œà measures the fractional loading, and the vacuum refractive index n = eœà
follows uniquely from the multiplicative composition law via Cauchy‚Äôs exponential functional equation. We interpret the full nonlinear DFD field equation as a stress‚Äìstrain constitutive equilibrium
of the vacuum medium, define gravitational strain and stress in analogy with continuum mechanics,
and show that the vacuum exhibits reduced gravitational permittivity at low gradients‚Äîthe mechanism behind flat rotation curves. The vacuum stiffness K0 = ‚ÑèH02 /(8œÄŒ±57 c) is parameter-free given
the master invariant and one measured scale. The constitutive split parameter Œ∫ = Œ±/4 ‚âà 1.82√ó10‚àí3
is derived from gauge-emergence physics, not fitted. We quantify the EM‚Äìœà coupling prediction,
address the vacuum energy hierarchy within a framework consistent with the Œ±57 suppression, and
survey the 114-year intellectual lineage from Einstein‚Äôs variable-c proposal through analog gravity
to the present framework.

I.

INTRODUCTION

The vacuum of quantum electrodynamics is not empty.
It has a permittivity Œµ0 and a permeability ¬µ0 , setting
‚àö
the speed of light c = 1/ Œµ0 ¬µ0 . In the Density Field Dynamics (DFD) framework, these are not fixed constants
of ‚Äúempty space‚Äù but constitutive properties of a physical
medium‚Äîthe quantum vacuum‚Äîwhose electromagnetic
response is modified by the presence of mass-energy.
DFD reproduces all solar system tests of GR [17, 18],
matches 175 galaxy rotation curves from the SPARC
database [19], and derives Œ±‚àí1 = 137.036 from Chern‚Äì
Simons quantization [16]. The proton‚Äôs rest mass is approximately 99% gluon field energy [20]‚Äîmass is concentrated energy localized within the vacuum. Lunar laser
ranging constrains |GÃá/G| < 10‚àí13 yr‚àí1 [21], consistent
with a fixed vacuum stiffness.
This paper develops the vacuum loading interpretation
of DFD, in which:
1. Mass deposits a fractional energy loading œà in the
vacuum, modifying its refractive index to n = eœà .
2. The vacuum resists deformation with a force scale
(stiffness) K0 = c4 /(8œÄG), the same coefficient that
appears in the Einstein field equations. The energy
density of the loaded vacuum is uœà = K0 |‚àáœà|2 .
3. The nonlinear DFD field equation emerges as the
mechanical equilibrium condition for this loaded
medium.

The paper is organized as follows. Section II defines
and derives the vacuum stiffness K0 . Section III develops
the constitutive relations. Section IV interprets the field

equation as a constitutive equilibrium condition. Section V quantifies the EM‚Äìœà coupling. Section VI presents
the self-consistent argument for n = eœà . Section VII
treats the nonlinear regime with stress‚Äìstrain language.
Section VIII relates DFD to prior work. Section IX discusses implications and open questions. The dimensional
consistency of all key equations is verified in Appendix A.

in the field variable). The composition law
n(œà1 + œà2 ) = n(œà1 ) ¬∑ n(œà2 )

Specifically, the functional equation f (x + y) =
f (x)f (y) with f (0) = 1 and f continuous and monotone
has the unique solution f (x) = eax for some a Ã∏= 0 [22].
Setting a = 1 by the normalization convention œà = ln n
gives n = eœà .
The physical content is that there is no pre-existing
medium with independent atomic structure. The vacuum
is the medium, and œà represents the total accumulated
loading‚Äînot a perturbation on some fixed background.
Unlike the Kerr effect in nonlinear optics (where n =
n0 + n2 I is additive because I is a small perturbation
on a pre-existing medium with baseline n0 ), the vacuum
loading is cumulative and self-reinforcing: the medium
through which the next increment of energy propagates
has already been modified by all previous loading.

Why the linear ansatz fails

VI.

WHY n = eœà : THE CAUCHY FUNCTIONAL
EQUATION
A.

The composition law

DFD postulates n = eœà as the vacuum refractive index. The field equation yields dn/dœà = n, whose unique
solution with n(0) = 1 is n = eœà . But why should the
vacuum‚Äôs response be multiplicative rather than additive?
The answer lies in the physics of cumulative loading.
Consider two successive loadings: first load the vacuum

1. Thermodynamic extensivity. The loading œà
is an intensive thermodynamic variable. For a
medium whose response depends only on the current state (not on the path), the partition function
factorizes over independent loadings: Z(œà1 + œà2 ) =
Z(œà1 )¬∑Z(œà2 ). Since n is determined by Z, the multiplicative law follows.
2. U(1) phase accumulation. In the microsector,
R
œà
the gauge-field phase: œï = n ds =

eœà ds. The exponential ensures that phase accumulated over a path is the product of contributions

4
from each infinitesimal segment, the unique form
compatible with the group structure of U(1) phase
rotations.
3. PPN consistency. With œà = ‚àí2Œ¶/c2 and n =
eœà , the expansion n2 = e2œà = 1 + 2œà + 2œà 2 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
automatically generates the correct PPN parameters Œ≥ = 1, Œ≤ = 1 with all higher-order terms
determined‚Äîno separate tuning at each PN order.

œÉ ‚â° K0 ¬µ(s) ‚àáœà,

Reduced gravitational permittivity at low
gradients

The function ¬µ(s) acts as a field-dependent gravitational permittivity. At high strain (s ‚â´ 1), ¬µ ‚Üí 1 and
the vacuum conducts gravitational flux at full Newtonian
strength. At low strain (s ‚â™ 1), ¬µ ‚âà s ‚Üí 0: the vacuum
becomes a poor conductor of gravitational flux.
This is the physical mechanism behind flat rotation
curves. By Gauss‚Äôs law applied to the DFD field equation, the total gravitational flux through a sphere of radius r around a mass M is fixed:

When ¬µ is small (low gradients, large r), the gradient
|‚àáœà| must be larger than the Newtonian value to carry

rather than the Newtonian 1/r2 . Since the acceleration
a = (c2 /2)|‚àáœà|, this yields v 2 = ra = const‚Äîa flat rotation curve.
The analogy is to a nonlinear dielectric whose permittivity drops at low field strengths. In such a material,
the same enclosed charge produces a larger electric field
at large distances than in a linear dielectric, because the
reduced permittivity cannot screen the flux as effectively.
The DFD vacuum behaves identically: reduced gravitational permittivity at low gradients amplifies the gravitational field relative to the Newtonian prediction, eliminating the need for dark matter.

VIII.

RELATION TO PRIOR WORK

The idea that gravity might arise from or be described
through the electromagnetic properties of the vacuum
has a long history.

A.

Historical foundations

Einstein [1] proposed in 1911 that the gravitational
field modifies the speed of light: c(Œ¶) = c0 (1 + Œ¶/c2 ).
This is the earliest explicit treatment of gravity as a variable refractive index. DFD‚Äôs n = eœà is the nonlinear
completion of Einstein‚Äôs linear formula.
Wilson [2] proposed that mass creates a spatially varying dielectric constant in the vacuum, a direct precursor
to DFD‚Äôs constitutive-relation approach.
Gordon [3] showed that EM waves in a moving dielectric obey an effective ‚Äúoptical metric.‚Äù DFD inverts this:
the optical metric is fundamental, and the ‚Äúmoving dielectric‚Äù is the vacuum œà-field.
Dicke [4] proposed that gravity could originate from
modifications to the vacuum‚Äôs electromagnetic properties, suggesting that the speed of light correlates with

5
the gravitational potential of the universe‚Äîthe closest
pre-DFD articulation of the vacuum loading concept.

B.

Induced gravity and emergent approaches

Sakharov [5] proposed that gravity emerges from vacuum quantum fluctuations‚Äî‚Äúinduced gravity.‚Äù The
Einstein‚ÄìHilbert action arises as a one-loop effective action from matter fields, providing a natural framework
for DFD‚Äôs claim that G (and hence K0 ) is determined by
vacuum mode structure.
Visser [10] reviewed Sakharov‚Äôs programme, showing
how G emerges from the spectrum of matter field fluctuations. The ‚Äústiffness‚Äù in Sakharov‚Äôs picture is precisely
K0 = c4 /(8œÄG).

descends. The spectral action on a product geometry
M 4 √ó F simultaneously produces the Einstein‚ÄìHilbert
gravitational action and the Standard Model gauge kinetic terms. DFD identifies F with CP 2 √ó S 3 and uses
specific heat-kernel coefficients to derive Œ±‚àí1 = 137.036.
Recent work by Garcƒ±ÃÅa-Compean et al. [14] and
De Rham et al. [15] extends the optical-gravity analogy
into the metamaterial regime.

IX.
A.

The vacuum energy hierarchy

The vacuum loading picture distinguishes three scales:
Scale

C.

Polarizable vacuum and optical approaches

de Felice [6] gave a systematic treatment of the gravitational field as an optical medium with position-dependent
refractive index, deriving lensing, redshift, and Shapiro
delay from the optical analogy. DFD elevates this from
analogy to fundamental dynamics.
Puthoff [9] developed a ‚Äúpolarizable vacuum‚Äù model in
which mass modifies the vacuum‚Äôs permittivity and permeability via a scalar function, reproducing linearized
GR. This is formally similar to DFD‚Äôs constitutive relations, though DFD provides a dynamical action principle
and microsector derivation.
Perlick [8] established the rigorous mathematical foundation for ray optics in curved spacetime via Fermat‚Äôs
principle.

D.

Analog gravity and vacuum energy

Volovik [11] demonstrated that superfluid 3 He-A provides an exact analog of gravity, electromagnetism, and
the Standard Model as collective modes of the quantum
vacuum. Crucially, Volovik showed that the vacuum energy computed from mode sums is cancelled by the equilibrium condition‚Äîdirectly supporting DFD‚Äôs distinction
between K0 (vacuum force scale) and œÅŒõ (residual strain),
and anticipating the Œ±57 suppression mechanism.
BarceloÃÅ, Liberati, and Visser [13] provided a comprehensive review of analog gravity models. Electromagnetic
analogs (dielectric media with gradient refractive index)
are discussed as gravitational simulators. DFD makes
the inverse claim: the gravitational œà-field is the physical refractive medium, not merely an analogy.

The critical distinction: K0 is the vacuum force scale

observed œÅŒõ ‚àº 10‚àí9 J/m3 is the residual loading, a
‚Äústrain‚Äù of order H02 /c2 ‚àº 10‚àí52 m‚àí2 . The vacuum loading picture provides a natural framework for understanding the vacuum energy hierarchy, consistent with the Œ±57
suppression: the physical microsector has only kmax = 60
modes, and the residual vacuum energy is suppressed by
Œ±57 ‚àº 10‚àí122 relative to the Planck scale, rather than
being set by a continuum mode sum up to MP .
This parallels Volovik‚Äôs argument [11] that in superfluid 3 He, the vacuum energy from mode sums vastly
overestimates the gravitational effect, because most of
the vacuum energy is cancelled by the equilibrium condition.

B.

The vacuum stiffness and the master invariant

Given the master invariant G ¬∑ ‚Ñè ¬∑ H02 /c5 = Œ±57 [16]
and one measured scale, K0 is parameter-free: K0 =
‚ÑèH02 /(8œÄŒ±57 c). Whether this expression admits an independent derivation from the microsector topology remains open.

C.

The chain of reasoning

The vacuum loading interpretation can be stated as a
single chain:
1. The vacuum is a storage medium with Œµ0 , ¬µ0 .
2. Mass loads the medium: œà = ln n.

Connes and Chamseddine [7, 12] developed the spectral action framework from which DFD‚Äôs Œ± derivation [16]

3. The composition law forces n = eœà (Cauchy).

6
4. The stiffness is topologically structured, with normalization set by H0 : K0 = ‚ÑèH02 /(8œÄŒ±57 c).
5. The cosmological constant is residual strain.
6. QFT overestimates by overcounting modes.
7. EM‚Äìœà coupling is predicted and testable.

D.

Experimental programme

The key predictions are:
‚Ä¢ SQMS Q-ratio: 0.52 ¬± 0.08 (DFD) vs. 3.60 (BCS).
‚Ä¢ LPI slope Œæ(Œ∫): species-dependent if Œ∫ Ã∏= 0.
‚Ä¢ TE/TM cavity asymmetry: ‚àº 2Œ∫œà frequency splitting, with Œ∫ = Œ±/4 giving ‚àº 10‚àí3 œà level splitting.
‚Ä¢ Polarization rotation through ‚àáœà gradients.

Open questions

1. The exact value of Œª ‚àí 1 requires computing the
EM‚Äìœà mode overlap on CP 2 √ó S 3 .
2. The sector-split parameter Œ∫ = Œ±/4 is derived from
the gauge-emergence auxiliary metric; experimental confirmation via TE/TM splitting would test
this prediction.
3. Time dependence of K0 : if G(t) varies with cosmic
epoch, implications for nucleosynthesis need quantification.
4. Whether the vacuum loading picture and
Sakharov‚Äôs programme can be unified through a
single spectral action calculation.

The œà-Screen Cosmology:
CMB Without Dark Matter from Density Field Dynamics
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher
gary@gtacompanies.com
December 25, 2025

Abstract
We present a complete cosmological framework within Density Field Dynamics (DFD)
where the CMB observations traditionally attributed to dark matter arise instead from the
œà-screen‚Äîthe accumulated variation of the scalar field œà along the line of sight. The peak
ratio R ‚â° H1 /H2 ‚âà 2.4 emerges from baryon loading alone, with the 1/¬µ enhancement
from œà-gravity canceling in the ratio. The peak location ‚Ñì1 ‚âà 220 arises from œà-lensing
(gradient-index optics) with ‚àÜœà = 0.30. We connect this cosmological framework to the
DFD microsector (CP2 √ó S 3 ), showing that the same topological structure that derives
Œ± = 1/137 and the fermion mass hierarchy also determines
cosmological observables through
‚àö
four parameter-free Œ±-relations: a0 /cH0 = 2 Œ± (MOND scale), kŒ± = Œ±2 /(2œÄ) (clock
coupling), ka = 3/(8Œ±) (self-coupling), and Œ∑c = Œ±/4 (EM threshold). The fourth relation
enables a new falsification test using SOHO/UVCS coronal observations: the predicted
multi-wavelength signature (O VI vs Ly-Œ± asymmetry ratio ‚âà 16) discriminates sharply
from standard physics (ratio ‚âà 1). Three independent ‚àÜœà estimators are defined, along
with three falsifiers: (1) CMB-LSS cross-correlation, (2) estimator closure, and (3) UVCS
multi-wavelength test. The framework eliminates dark matter and dark energy as physical
entities, replacing them with optical effects in the œà-universe.

Introduction: Cosmology as Inverse Optics

1.1

The Paradigm Shift

Standard cosmology treats the CMB as a pristine snapshot of the early universe, analyzed
using General Relativity with ŒõCDM parameters. This forward-modeling approach has been
remarkably successful but requires two unexplained components: cold dark matter (‚Ñ¶c ‚âà 0.26)
and dark energy (‚Ñ¶Œõ ‚âà 0.69).
Density Field Dynamics (DFD) proposes a fundamentally different approach: cosmology
as an inverse optical problem. The primary unknown is not a set of cosmological parameters
but a reconstructed field‚Äîthe œà-screen:
‚àÜœà(z, nÃÇ) ‚â° œàem (z, nÃÇ) ‚àí œàobs

(1)

This screen encodes the cumulative optical effect of the scalar field œà along each line of
sight. What standard cosmology interprets as ‚Äúdark matter effects‚Äù and ‚Äúcosmic acceleration‚Äù
are reinterpreted as optical phenomena in the œà-universe.

1.2

physics parameters also determines cosmological observables. This is not a coincidence‚Äîit is
the unifying principle of DFD.

location.

today?‚Äù remains.
2. Inflation: Early-universe dynamics (inflation, reheating, baryogenesis) are outside current scope. DFD describes the œà-universe; primordial physics is separate.
3. Tensor modes: Primordial gravitational waves and their effect on B-mode polarization
in œà-cosmology not yet analyzed.

Tree-Level No-Drive Theorem for the Minimal Optical-Metric EM Sector in Density
Field Dynamics:
Œªbare = 1 from the Pure Gauge-Invariant Action
Gary Alcock1
1

Independent Researcher, Los Angeles, CA, USA‚àó
(Dated: May 2, 2026)

In Density Field Dynamics (DFD), the parameter Œª introduced in Appendix R of the unified
paper controls whether laboratory electromagnetic fields can pump the scalar field œà at frequency
2œâ beyond the optical-metric forward-propagation channel. Appendix R is broader than a single
baseline: it contains a minimal tree-level optical-metric sector, a geometry-restoration channel,
threshold-sensitive nonlinear structure, and a dual-sector Œ∫ extension. Here we address the minimal
sector only. We show that in the minimal tree-level optical-metric EM sector of DFD, the linear-in-œà
EM source produced by the gauge-invariant action is proportional to the EM energy density (E 2 /c2 +
B 2 )/(2¬µ0 ) rather than the stress invariant (E 2 /c2 ‚àíB 2 )/(2¬µ0 ). For ideal standing-wave cavity modes
with energy equipartition, the energy-density source has no 2œâ component after volume integration.
Consequently the bare-coupling value of the back-reaction parameter equals unity at tree level in the
minimal sector, which we write as Œªbare = 1. The effective laboratory inference Œªeff differs from Œªbare
by explicit corrections: a classical 1/Q mimicking effect from finite cavity quality factor, geometryrestoration contributions for non-symmetric or TE+TM superposition modes, threshold corrections
if the system approaches Œ∑c = Œ±/4, and any contributions from constitutive-sector splitting (the
Œ∫ channel) or from beyond-baseline dimension-5 operators Œæ œà F¬µŒΩ F ¬µŒΩ . We therefore reinterpret
Appendix R‚Äôs accidental bound |Œª ‚àí 1| < 3 √ó 10‚àí5 and the projected intentional-search reach
|Œª ‚àí 1| ‚àº 10‚àí14 as constraints on these beyond-minimal-sector channels: the minimal sector‚Äôs
Œªbare = 1 is the null baseline, and nonzero inferred Œªeff diagnoses which beyond-baseline channel is
active.

INTRODUCTION

rapidly oscillating EM fields, in turn, actively pump œà
modes beyond the standard contribution of their energy
density to the matter source?
Appendix R of [1] introduces a phenomenological parameter Œª to quantify this back-reaction. The parameter
enters the single-mode reduction of the œà equation of
motion as the coefficient of an EM-driven source term:

Corollary 6 (Inferred-Œª decomposition). For a laboratory experiment fitting the Appendix R mode equation (1)
to cavity observations, the inferred parameter Œªeff decomposes as
Œªeff ‚àí 1 = Œ¥Q + Œ¥geom + Œ¥thr + Œ¥Œ∫ + Œ¥Œæ ,

(24)

where Œ¥Q ‚àº 1/Q is the finite-Q equipartition-violation
mimic, Œ¥geom arises from geometry-restoration channels
(non-ideal or TE+TM superposition modes, asymmetric cavity geometries), Œ¥thr arises only if Œ∑ approaches
Œ∑c , Œ¥Œ∫ arises from dual-sector constitutive splitting (the
Œ∫ channel of [1], Appendix R), and Œ¥Œæ arises from any
beyond-baseline dimension-5 operator Œæ œà F¬µŒΩ F ¬µŒΩ . Each
channel is separately analyzed in Section VI and, in
principle, separately constrainable by cavity-geometry, Qscaling, threshold-approach, and constitutive-splitting experiments.
Corollary 7 (Reinterpretation of existing cavity
bounds). The Appendix R accidental bound |Œª ‚àí 1| <
3 √ó 10‚àí5 from cavity stability is not a constraint on a
single intrinsic DFD parameter but a joint constraint on
|Œ¥Q + Œ¥geom + Œ¥thr + Œ¥Œ∫ + Œ¥Œæ |. Similarly the projected
intentional-search reach ‚àº 10‚àí14 is a joint bound. Separating the channels requires the experimental protocols
described in Section VI.
VI.

DISCUSSION

The inferred-Œª decomposition Eq. (24) has five distinct
channels. We analyze each in turn.
A.

Œ¥Q : Finite-Q equipartition-violation mimic

For a real cavity with quality factor Q, equipartition
of electric and magnetic energy holds only to fractional
accuracy 1/Q. The deviation produces a small residual
2œâ component
in the volume-integrated energy-density
R
source u (E 2 /c2 +B 2 ) d3 r that was required to vanish in
Corollary 4. The residual mimics a back-reaction drive:
Œ¥Q ‚àº

1
.
Q

Œ¥Q is distinguishable from genuine beyond-baseline
channels by Q-scaling tests: a true ŒæœàF 2 operator produces a Q-independent signal (up to overall amplitude),
while Œ¥Q scales as 1/Q.

B.

Œ¥geom : Geometry-restoration channels

Theorem 5 covers only the ideal symmetric single-mode
standing-wave case (hypothesis (iv)). Appendix R of [1]
explicitly discusses geometry configurations outside that
idealization which restore driven overlap:
‚Ä¢ TE+TM superposition modes. A cavity excited in a mixed TE+TM mode has different spatial profiles for |E0 |2 and |B0 |2 that do not satisfy
the pure equipartition structure of a single mode.
The integrated 2œâ component of (E 2 /c2 +B 2 ) need
not vanish for such superpositions.
‚Ä¢ Asymmetric cavity geometries. Geometries
symmetry can produce nonzero
Rwith broken2 spatial
u(r) (|E0 | /c2 ‚àí |B0 |2 )(r) d3 r for the particular
test function u(r) of the œà mode, even when the
globally integrated equipartition holds.
‚Ä¢ Mode beating. Two or more cavity modes
with commensurate frequencies can produce timevarying profiles that do not individually satisfy the
standing-wave assumption (ii) of Lemma 3.
Each
of these restores nonzero overlap G
‚â°
R
u(r) ŒûÃÇ2œâ (r) d3 r (in the notation of Appendix R
Eq. (R3)) and produces a nonzero effective drive proportional to Œ¥geom . We stress that these restored-overlap
channels are produced by the same minimal opticalmetric action Eq. (6): the cancellation Corollary 4
that gives Œªbare = 1 only covered the ideal symmetric
single-mode standing-wave case of hypothesis (iv),
so leaving that idealization does not introduce any
new operators, it only drops the specific cancellation
condition. The minimal-sector action can, and in nonsymmetric geometries does, produce a 2œâ drive through
the energy-density source without any beyond-baseline
coupling being present.
Experimental isolation of Œ¥geom uses cavity-geometry
scans: the signal should vary with cavity shape and mode
content in a calculable way, while Œ¥Œæ (a genuine beyondbaseline operator) would not.

C.

Œ¥thr : Threshold physics

(25)

For state-of-the-art superconducting cavities with Q ‚àº
1010 -1011 , Œ¥Q ‚àº 10‚àí10 -10‚àí11 . The existing accidental bound 3 √ó 10‚àí5 is comfortably above this classical
background; the projected intentional-search reach 10‚àí14
is well below it, so distinguishing Œ¥Q from the other
channels is an experimental requirement for sub-10‚àí10
searches.

For laboratory cavities with Œ∑ ‚àº 10‚àí14 ‚â™ Œ∑c =
Œ±/4 ‚àº 10‚àí3 , the nonlinear kG threshold coupling ([1],
Appendix G, Theorem G.2) is inactive and Œ¥thr ‚âà 0. For
astrophysical plasmas where Œ∑ approaches or exceeds Œ∑c
(coronal mass ejections, laboratory fusion plasmas, etc.),
nonlinear activation can produce effective Œª Ã∏= 1 through
the threshold channel. This is outside the hypotheses of
Theorem 5.

5
D.

Œ¥Œ∫ : Dual-sector constitutive splitting

This distinction is critical and deserves emphasis.
Appendix R of [1] introduces a dual-sector extension
with parameter Œ∫ governing electric-magnetic constitutive splitting: in that extension, the effective permittivity œµ and permeability ¬µ respond differently to œà, rather
than both being set by the single optical refractive index n = eœà . Specifically the minimal sector uses one
global n for both polarizations of the EM field, while the
Œ∫ extension allows distinct responses for E and B.
This is structurally different from the Œª channel:
‚Ä¢ Œª controls whether a direct (E 2 /c2 ‚àí B 2 )œà source
appears in the Lagrangian, producing an inhomogeneous drive of the œà mode.
‚Ä¢ Œ∫ controls whether the effective optical response
splits between E-mode and B-mode, producing a
modification of the dispersion relation rather than
a drive.
Both parameters are phenomenologically present in
full Appendix R but are independent degrees of freedom. A dual-sector constitutive splitting (Œ∫ Ã∏= 0) in
general produces unequal E 2 and B 2 response spatial
profiles, which breaks equipartition at the sector level
and generates a nonzero effective Œ¥Œ∫ even in the nominally below-threshold regime. The minimal-sector theorem of this paper fixes Œªbare = 1 under the assumption Œ∫ = 0. A genuine measurement of Œªeff Ã∏= 1 therefore does not automatically indicate a ŒæœàF 2 operator: it
could instead indicate activation of the Œ∫ channel. Distinguishing the two requires independent measurement of
Œ∫ through polarization-sensitive or E/B-asymmetric observables. This is a separate experimental program from
the direct-drive cavity test.
The present theorem therefore does not overturn Appendix R. It establishes the null baseline for one of Appendix R‚Äôs coupling channels, conditional on the others
being inactive or held fixed.

E.

Œ¥Œæ : Beyond-baseline dimension-5 operator

Any effective Lagrangian of the form LŒæ = Œæ œà F¬µŒΩ F ¬µŒΩ
is gauge-invariant and general-covariant in the optical
metric, and is not excluded on symmetry grounds. It
is, however, not produced by the minimal optical-metric
action Eq. (6) under the conventions of Lemma 1: that
Lemma exhibits the full œà-dependence of the minimal action, and it contains no œà F 2 cross-term at linear order.
Any nonzero Œæ is therefore a beyond-baseline operator.
Œ¥Œæ is the channel corresponding to dilaton-type coupling familiar from scalar-tensor gravity and stringtheoretic dilaton models. Measuring Œ¥Œæ separately from
Œ¥Œ∫ , Œ¥Q , and Œ¥geom is a genuinely new experimental question: it would constrain the presence of a dimension-5

coupling not required by the DFD postulates P1 and P2
alone.
F.

Experimental isolation protocol

To separate the five channels in Eq. (24), an experimental program would include:
1. Q-scaling series to isolate Œ¥Q (scales as 1/Q, other
channels do not);
2. Geometry and mode-content variation to map
Œ¥geom predictions;
3. Œ∑-varying series (through cavity energy or wall density control) to test threshold behavior Œ¥thr ;
4. Polarization-sensitive measurements to characterize Œ¥Œ∫ independently;
5. Universal residual after the above subtractions, if
present, constrains Œ¥Œæ .
The minimal-sector theorem of this paper is an input
to such a program, not a replacement for it.

VII.

SUMMARY

We have proved a tree-level no-drive theorem for the
minimal optical-metric EM sector in DFD: under DFD
postulates P1 and P2, the gauge-invariant action Eq. (6)
with no added operators, below-threshold conditions,
ideal standing-wave equipartition, and mode symmetry,
the bare-coupling value of the Appendix R back-reaction
parameter is unity at tree level, Œªbare = 1. The derivation uses only the œà-expansion of the minimal action and
the Fourier content of standing-wave fields.
The effective laboratory inference Œªeff decomposes into
five additive channels (Corollary 6), none of which is zero
a priori in a real experiment. In particular, a nonzero
Œªeff ‚àí 1 does not automatically diagnose beyond-DFD
physics: it may reflect finite-Q equipartition violation
(Œ¥Q ), geometry-restoration channels via TE+TM superposition or cavity asymmetry (Œ¥geom ), threshold physics
approaching Œ∑c (Œ¥thr ), dual-sector constitutive splitting
(Œ¥Œ∫ ), or a genuine dimension-5 operator ŒæœàF¬µŒΩ F ¬µŒΩ (Œ¥Œæ ).
The Appendix R accidental bound |Œª ‚àí 1| < 3 √ó 10‚àí5
and the projected intentional-search reach ‚àº 10‚àí14 are
joint bounds on these channels. Separating them experimentally requires cavity-geometry variation, Q-scaling,
threshold-approach, and polarization-sensitive protocols,
outlined in Section VI.
The theorem therefore does not overturn Appendix R
or eliminate Œª as a phenomenological parameter. It identifies the null baseline within one specific channel and
clarifies what a nonzero inferred Œªeff would and would not
imply. This is a narrower claim than ‚Äúpure DFD predicts
Œª = 1 exactly,‚Äù which our earlier framing sometimes suggested. The narrower claim is what the minimal-sector
action actually supports.

Two Numerical Relations Linking the Fine-Structure Constant
to Gravitational Phenomenology
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher
gary@gtacompanies.com
December 4, 2025

Abstract
We report two numerical relations connecting the fine-structure constant Œ± ‚âà‚àö1/137 to
gravitational phenomenology. First, the MOND acceleration scale satisfies a0 = 2 Œ± cH0 to
within the current uncertainty in H0 , where c is the speed of light and H0 is the Hubble parameter.
Second, if atomic clock responses to gravitational potential variations are parameterized as

‚àö the numerical evidence, offer a vertex-counting
heuristic that motivates the appearance of Œ± and Œ±2 , and identify falsifiable predictions for
near-term clock experiments. A six-month optical clock campaign currently underway should
confirm or exclude the predicted kŒ± at > 10œÉ significance.

Vertex-Counting Heuristic

‚àö
Why might Œ± appear in the MOND relation
and Œ±2 in the clock relation? We offer a heuristic based on QED vertex counting. A formal
derivation within the DFD framework is given in
Ref. [15].
In quantum electrodynamics, each interaction
‚àö
vertex contributes a factor of Œ± to the amplitude. If electromagnetically bound matter couples to a scalar field through QED-like vertices,
‚àö
the coupling strength scales as ( Œ±)n where n is
the number of vertices.

a vertex-counting structure, and this structure
yields falsifiable predictions.

Two Numerical Relations Linking the Fine-Structure Constant
to Gravitational Phenomenology
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher
gary@gtacompanies.com
December 3, 2025

Abstract
We highlight two numerical relations connecting the fine-structure constant Œ± ‚âà
‚àö1/137 to
gravitational phenomenology. First, the MOND acceleration scale satisfies a0 = 2 Œ± cH0 to
within the current uncertainty in H0 , where c is the speed of light and H0 is the Hubble parameter.
Second, if atomic clock responses to gravitational potential variations are parameterized as
Œ±
Œ±
KA = kŒ± SA
, where SA
are tabulated Œ±-sensitivity coefficients, then existing clock data are
consistent with kŒ± = Œ±2 /(2œÄ) within current ‚àº 2œÉ uncertainties. These relations involve no free
parameters: given Œ± and H0 , both a0 and kŒ± are fixed. We present
the numerical evidence, offer
‚àö
a vertex-counting heuristic that motivates the appearance of Œ± and Œ±2 , and identify falsifiable
predictions for near-term clock experiments. A multi-month optical clock campaign building on
recent cavity-referenced work should be able to confirm or exclude the predicted kŒ± at > 10œÉ
significance.

Draft version September 30, 2025
Typeset using LATEX twocolumn style in AASTeX631

Unexplained Bright‚ÄìDim Intensity Asymmetries in SOHO/UVCS Lyman-Œ± Data
Gary Alcock1
1 Los Angeles, California, USA

ABSTRACT
We present a permutation test analysis of 334 daily sequences of SOHO/UVCS Lyman-Œ± spectra
(2007‚Äì2009). By splitting frames into bright and dim subsets and comparing their median intensity
and wavelength, we test whether observed differences exceed those expected from random sampling.
We find that 163 of 321 day‚Äìradial bins (51%) exhibit statistically significant bright‚Äìdim intensity
contrasts at false discovery rate (FDR) 5%. The effect is modest (Cohen‚Äôs d ‚âà 0.24) but robust to
permutation. Velocity differences are not significant (d ‚âà ‚àí0.03). The origin of this asymmetry is
unknown: it may reflect solar structures, instrumental systematics, or unexplored physical processes.
We publish these results as an open anomaly to encourage community investigation.

Keywords: solar corona ‚Äî spectroscopy ‚Äî statistical methods ‚Äî ultraviolet astronomy
1. INTRODUCTION

The solar corona exhibits complex dynamics observable through ultraviolet spectroscopy. The SOHO Ultraviolet Coronagraph Spectrometer (UVCS; Kohl et al.
1995, 1997; Raymond et al. 1997) provides long time‚Äì
series of Lyman-Œ± observations. Instrumental stability
has been studied in depth (Gardner et al. 2002; Strachan et al. 2002; Guhathakurta et al. 1999; Kohl et al.
2006), but statistical anomalies may persist in archival
data. Here we document bright‚Äìdim intensity asymmetries in UVCS daily sequences using a nonparametric,
permutation-based approach with multiple-testing control.
2. DATA AND METHODS

We analyzed 150,685 frames from 895 UVCS exposures (2007‚Äì2009). Frames were binned by day and
projected radius (Rbin = 0.0 corresponds to the innermost detector bin, ‚âà 1.5‚Äì1.6 R‚äô ). For each (day,Rbin )
with ‚â• 2 frames, we sorted frames by total line intensity
and split at the median, with the upper half designated
‚Äúbright‚Äù and lower half ‚Äúdim‚Äù. This median-split definition replaces earlier descriptions of ‚Äúrandom partitioning‚Äù to ensure reproducibility.
For each group we computed:

Permutation tests (20,000 replicates per group; Ernst
2004; Good 2013) generated null distributions; two-sided
p-values were corrected by Benjamini‚ÄìHochberg FDR
(Benjamini & Hochberg 1995). Effect sizes were quantified with Cohen‚Äôs d (Cohen 1988). Instrumental filters
used in exploratory work (‚àí0.9 < ‚àÜI < 20, |‚àÜv| < 120
km s‚àí1 ) were not applied in the final analysis; results
reported here use the full data without post-hoc rejection.
Data quality notes: Daily frame counts ranged from
2 to 150 (median ‚àº 20). We verified that all exposures
passed UVCS quality flags as archived by the SOHO
Science Archive (SSA). Known pointing offsets and detector artifacts are logged in Gardner et al. (2002), but
no anomalies specific to the intervals studied (2007‚Äì2009
solar minimum) were documented.
2.1. Pre-registered flagged-day definition
To avoid circularity, we pre-registered a binary
‚Äúflagged‚Äù label (hereafter ‚Äúflagged (significant) days‚Äù)
for external-validation analyses: a day is flagged if the
permutation test for intensity contrast at Rbin = 0.0
yields a two-sided p < 0.05 (before any comparison to
external solar activity catalogs). This label is then used
once, as an independent predictor in the CME coincidence analysis below.

1. Intensity contrast ‚àÜI = (Ibright /Idim ) ‚àí 1,
2. Wavelength shift ‚àÜŒª = Œªbright ‚àí Œªdim ,
3. Doppler velocity ‚àÜv = c ‚àÜŒª/Œª0 , with Œª0 =
1215.67 AÃä.

2.2. CME time+angle coincidence scoring
We assessed external validity by cross-matching UVCS
observing windows with LASCO CMEs (Brueckner et
al. 1995; Yashiro et al. 2004). For each UVCS day

2

Alcock

we constructed a binary indicator aligned any that
is 1 if a cataloged CME occurred within a temporal
padding window (pad ‚àà {0, 30, 60, 120} minutes) of the
UVCS observing interval and within an angular tolerance (tol ‚àà {0‚ó¶ , 5‚ó¶ , 10‚ó¶ , 15‚ó¶ , 20‚ó¶ , 30‚ó¶ }) of the UVCS slit
position angle. We then computed the aligned any
rate separately for (i) all days and (ii) the flagged
subset (Sec. 2.1), and summarized the rate difference
(flagged minus all) across the (pad, tol) grid. Uncertainty and enrichment significance were gauged with
Fisher‚Äôs exact test on the 2 √ó 2 table of hits/non-hits
in the two groups. The per-cell results are packaged in
cme timewindow significance grid.csv (Data Availability).
Note on GOES flare coincidence. We attempted an
analogous analysis using daily SWPC text files for
GOES flares. For 2007‚Äì2009, the specific daily endpoints we probed returned unavailable (HTTP 404) or
empty files, yielding no usable lines. Because this epoch
lies in the deep solar minimum and the archive access
was incomplete for the daily text products, we treat flare
coincidence as non-informative here and rely on CMEs
as the independent coronal activity proxy.
3. RESULTS

Out of 321 testable day‚Äìradius groups, 163 passed the
5% FDR threshold for intensity contrast. Figure 2 shows
the permutation p-value spectrum, indicating clear departure from the uniform null. Figure 3 displays intensity contrast versus velocity shift; significant cases cluster at nonzero contrast but near-zero ‚àÜv. Figure 4 compares observed and null distributions. Figure 5 shows
the temporal distribution of significant cases; a compact
year-by-month view appears in Table 2.
Why only Rbin = 0.0? The UVCS campaign density
during 2007‚Äì2009 yields robust sample sizes at the innermost bin only. Higher Rbin locations did not provide
sufficient (day,bin) groups with ‚â• 2 frames to support
a reliable permutation test and FDR control; counts
are summarized in Appendix A. We therefore report
Rbin = 0.0 as the adequately powered subset and document the absence of higher-radius detections for completeness.
Overall effect sizes: contrast d = 0.24 (small‚Äì
medium), velocity d = ‚àí0.03 (null). A simple power
analysis indicates that N ‚â≥ 10 frames per day are required for robust detection at d ‚âà 0.24 with Œ± = 0.05
(Faul et al. 2007). A frame-count histogram is provided
in Figure 6 to visualize sampling depth across days.
3.1. External validity: CME coincidence

Figure 1. External validation via LASCO CME coincidence. Heatmap shows absolute enrichment in CME
time+angle alignment rates (flagged minus all) as a function
of time pad (minutes) and angular tolerance (degrees). Cell
annotations display the enrichment values (fractional units).
A color bar indicates the magnitude and sign. Per-cell twosided Fisher p-values and permutation p-values are supplied
in the CSV cme timewindow significance grid.csv.

Flagged (significant) days (Sec. 2.1) exhibit consistently higher CME time+angle coincidence than the
full set across the (pad, tol) grid. Figure 1 summarizes
the enrichment as absolute rate difference (flagged minus all); enrichment is positive in all displayed cells,
with typical magnitudes ‚àº0.10 for pad= 0 min and
‚àº0.15‚Äì0.25 for pads of 30‚Äì120 min. As a representative configuration (pad= 60 min, tol= 10‚ó¶ ), the baseline
CME-coincidence rate for all days is 0.606 (206/340),
the flagged-day rate is 0.786 (11/14), yielding an absolute enrichment of +0.180; Fisher‚Äôs exact two-sided
p = 0.263 and a label-shuffle permutation p = 0.265 for
the same 2 √ó 2 margins. Across the full 4 √ó 6 pad√ótol
grid, all 24 cells show positive enrichment; a binomial
sign test against random ¬± signs gives p ‚âà (1/2)24 ‚âÉ
6 √ó 10‚àí8 (p < 10‚àí6 ). The mean and median enrichments across the 24 cells are both in the +0.2‚Äì+0.3
range (see CSV). The number of flagged days is modest
(n = 14 at raw p < 0.05), which limits per-cell power,
so we emphasize the grid-level consistency rather than
any single-cell p-value. The high baseline coincidence
(e.g., ‚àº60% at pad= 60 min, tol= 10‚ó¶ ) likely reflects a
combination of LASCO catalog completeness and generous time/angle windows; the additional +18 percentage
points in flagged days indicates a substantial relative lift
rather than a floor effect, suggesting their asymmetries
are systematically linked to CME occurrence rather than
random chance.

(clean subset). Significant cases cluster at nonzero contrast
while ‚àÜv ‚âÉ 0.

new physics.
4. DISCUSSION

The bright‚Äìdim contrast signal is statistically robust,
yet its physical origin is uncertain. Possible explanations
include: (1) solar structures (e.g., streamers, CME remnants; Ofman et al. 1997; Raymond et al. 1997; Strachan
et al. 2002); (2) instrumental systematics (slit illumination, vignetting, detector response drift; Gardner et al.

4.1. Physical link between CMEs and bright‚Äìdim
asymmetries
CMEs introduce strong, evolving density and topology
changes in the low corona that can modulate LymanŒ± brightness via resonant scattering and line-of-sight
geometry. Density enhancements and altered illumi-

4

Alcock

Figure 4. Observed vs. null distributions of contrast and
Doppler velocity. Observed contrasts are systematically
higher.

to accommodate (i) CME onset/identification timing in
catalogs and (ii) propagation to 1.5‚Äì1.6 R‚äô . Typical
CME speeds of ‚àº300‚Äì1200 km s‚àí1 imply transit times
of order 15‚Äì60 minutes across several tenths of R‚äô in the
low corona, though acceleration phases and projection
effects broaden this range (Zhang & Dere 2006; Temmer 2021). The positive enrichment at modest pads
suggests the asymmetry co-occurs with, or shortly precedes/follows, cataloged CME activity; the effect does
not require precise timing at the minute level to be detectable in our daily aggregates.
4.3. Negative controls and selection considerations

Figure 5. Timeline of significant bright‚Äìdim asymmetries
(CLEAN FDR 5%). Clustering is evident during specific
months.

To probe selection effects, we performed label-shuffle
controls that preserve the number of flagged days and
their epoch distribution (month-level counts) and recomputed the pad√ótol grid. The resulting enrichment
centered near zero (as expected under the null), supporting that the observed CME coincidence is not an artifact
of epoch-dependent catalog completeness. We also note
that the CME method compares flagged days directly
to the full set drawn from the same calendar interval
(2007‚Äì2009), mitigating long-term activity-cycle confounds. Further geometric controls (e.g., cross-checks
of LASCO C2 position angles relative to the UVCS slit)
are natural extensions for future work.
4.4. Testable Hypotheses and Diagnostics
Specific hypotheses worth testing include:
‚Ä¢ Instrumental: correlation with detector temperature telemetry; slit position and spacecraft roll
angle.
‚Ä¢ Solar: coincidence with streamer locations from
LASCO C2 coronagraph images; periods of elevated solar wind speed (e.g., ACE/SWICS).

Figure 6. Distribution of frame counts per day (all Rbin ).
Vertical line marks median (‚àº20).

nation anisotropy can bias median-split intensity partitions without requiring large net Doppler shifts, consistent with the near-zero ‚àÜv we observe. CME-driven
changes to the ambient streamer belt and sheath structures can therefore produce bright‚Äìdim intensity asymmetries even when velocity centroids remain stable (e.g.,
Yashiro et al. 2004; Strachan et al. 2002).
4.2. Timing and causality
Our coincidence pads (0‚Äì120 min) are symmetric
around each UVCS observing window and are intended

‚Ä¢ Orbital/temporal: possible 6-month SOHO orbital periodicity; possible 27-day solar rotation cycle.
‚Ä¢ Effect-size linkage (prediction): test whether
larger ‚àÜI correlates with closer CME timing
and/or smaller angle offsets; a positive association
would further support a causal connection.
Future work should extend analysis across all radial bins
as data permit, and perform joint correlations with solar activity indices (sunspot number, F10.7 flux). Engagement with the UVCS instrument team is essential,
as they may immediately recognize these signatures as
known systematics or observing-program effects.

5. CONCLUSIONS

We report unexplained bright‚Äìdim asymmetries in
UVCS Lyman-Œ± data. The signal is statistically significant but modest, and lacks corresponding velocity
shifts. We release these results to the community as an
anomaly for further investigation.
DATA AVAILABILITY
The SOHO/UVCS data analyzed here are publicly
available from the SOHO Science Archive (SSA). Derived tables used in the figures and appendices are
included in the supplementary package (uvcs perm
results.csv, uvcs perm clean significant.csv, uvcs perm
clean top.csv, and summary CSVs in paper/data/). All
figure assets referenced in this manuscript (uvcs pvals
hist.png, uvcs contrast vs dv.png, uvcs obs vs null.png,
uvcs significant timeline.png, uvcs significant monthly.
png) are provided.

For the external-validation analysis, we include cme
alignment grid.png (Fig. 1) and the underlying grid table cme timewindow significance grid.csv with columns
{pad min, tol deg, k all, n all, rate all, k flag, n flag,
rate flag, enrichment, p fisher two sided, p perm two
sided}. Daily GOES flare text archives were unavailable
for this epoch under the queried endpoints, so flare coincidence products are not used. The negative-control figure cme label shuffle null.png (Fig. 8) is also provided.
ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
I thank the SOHO/UVCS instrument team (J. L.
Kohl, L. Strachan, J. C. Raymond, and collaborators)
for making the UVCS data publicly available and for
their foundational instrument papers. I also thank colleagues who provided methodological feedback during
the development of this analysis. SOHO is a project of
international cooperation between ESA and NASA.

APPENDIX

A. MULTI-RADIUS ANALYSIS

Although the analysis emphasized Rbin = 0.0, we queried additional projected radii. The number of day‚Äìradial
groups and fraction significant at FDR 5% are summarized below; only the innermost bin had sufficient groups to
support robust inference in 2007‚Äì2009.

B. TEMPORAL CORRELATIONS

We compared the timeline of significant asymmetries (Figure 5) to monthly counts (Table 2) to visualize clustering; a
fuller treatment against external indices (sunspot number, F10.7, SOHO orbital phase) is left to follow-up. Preliminary
inspection shows weak clustering during mid-2008, not obviously aligned with sunspot activity.

C. NEGATIVE-CONTROL SHUFFLE TEST FOR CME ENRICHMENT

We visualize the label-shuffle null for the CME enrichment grid as a histogram of mean enrichments across the 24
cells (20,000 shuffles).

6

Alcock

Figure 7. Monthly distribution of CLEAN FDR-significant bright‚Äìdim asymmetries.

Figure 8. Label-shuffle negative control for the CME enrichment grid. We randomly permute the ‚Äúflagged‚Äù labels (preserving
count and month-level distribution), recompute the pad√ótol enrichment in each of the 24 cells, and plot the resulting distribution
of cell-wise enrichments (gray histogram; 20,000 shuffles). The distribution is centered near zero with a narrow spread, as
expected under the null. Vertical solid (dashed) lines mark the observed mean (median) enrichment from the real labels; both
lie well outside the shuffle spread, complementing the sign test in Sec. 3.1.

Uniqueness of the Internal Manifold:
Deriving CP 2 √ó S 3 from Vacuum Axioms
in Density Field Dynamics
GARY THOMAS ALCOCK
(Dated: March 31, 2026)
Abstract. We prove that CP 2 √ó S 3 is the unique internal manifold for the spectral completion of Density Field Dynamics (DFD), under six physically motivated axioms on the
œà-vacuum. The axioms encode chirality (an empirical fact about nature), multiplicative vacuum composition (a direct consequence of DFD‚Äôs core postulate P1), ground-state stability,
and minimality. They do not reference the Standard Model gauge group, fermion content,
or any coupling constant. The gauge group SU(3) √ó SU(2) √ó U(1), three generations, and
Œ±‚àí1 = 137.036 emerge as derived consequences. The product structure K = KC √ó KG is
not assumed but forced by the logical incompatibility of the chirality requirement (w2 Ã∏= 0)
with Lie-group parallelizability (w2 = 0) on a single connected manifold.

1. Introduction
Density Field Dynamics [1] is a scalar refractive-index theory of gravity defined by two
postulates:
P1. The optical refractive index of the vacuum is n = eœà , where œà(x, t) is a scalar field
on flat R3,1 .
P2. Test bodies move under the conservative potential Œ¶ = ‚àíc2 œà/2.
The DFD microsector‚Äîwhich derives Œ± = 1/137, the fermion mass spectrum, and the
MOND interpolation function ¬µ(x) = x/(1+x)‚Äîrequires a spectral completion on a product
geometry M = R3,1 √ó K, where K is a compact internal manifold.
Previous versions of DFD postulated K = CP 2 √ó S 3 . This paper derives it from six
physically motivated axioms.
2. The Six Vacuum Axioms
Axiom 1 (Spectral completion). The œà-vacuum admits a UV completion via a spectral
action
(1)

SB = Tr f (D2 /Œõ2 )

on a product geometry M = R3,1 √ó K, where K is a compact Riemannian manifold, D is
the Dirac operator on M , Œõ is a UV cutoff, and f is a positive test function.
Motivation. The Chamseddine‚ÄìConnes spectral action [2] is the canonical trace functional
in noncommutative geometry that produces both the Einstein‚ÄìHilbert gravitational action
and gauge kinetic terms from a single principle. DFD‚Äôs postulates P1‚ÄìP2 emerge as the
weak-field limit of its a4 Seeley‚ÄìDeWitt coefficient.
Date: March 31, 2026.
1

2

GARY THOMAS ALCOCK

Axiom 2 (KaÃàhler chirality). At least one irreducible factor of K (in the de Rham decomposition) is a compact, simply-connected, KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein manifold with w2 Ã∏= 0 (spinc but
not spin).
Motivation. Nature violates parity [3]. Chiral fermions in 4D require a nonzero spinc Dirac
index on the internal space. The Hirzebruch‚ÄìRiemann‚ÄìRoch theorem‚Äîwhich computes
this
‚àö index‚àóas an integral of Chern characters‚Äîrequires the Dirac operator to decompose as
2(‚àÇ¬Ø + ‚àÇ¬Ø ). This decomposition takes its cleanest canonical form in the KaÃàhler setting,
where the spinc Dirac operator and the Dolbeault operator coincide. The condition w2 Ã∏= 0
(spinc -not-spin) ensures the index is non-trivially chiral: in the specific classification relevant
here (compact simply-connected positive-curvature KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein manifolds of complex
dimension ‚â§ 2), the spin candidates have vanishing chiral index, while the spinc -not-spin
candidate CP 2 does not.
Axiom 3 (Topological composition). At least one irreducible factor of K is a compact,
simply-connected Lie group manifold.
Motivation. DFD‚Äôs postulate P1 implies multiplicative composition of vacuum sectors:
ntotal = n1 ¬∑n2 . This composition is associative, has an identity (n = 1, i.e., œà = 0), and every
element has an inverse (n ‚Üí 1/n). In the spectral completion (V1), the internal degrees of
freedom at each spacetime point form a fiber. For the vacuum composition law to be fiberwise consistent‚Äîmeaning that the composition of two vacuum configurations at the same
spacetime point yields a valid vacuum configuration‚Äîthe fiber must carry a group structure.
A compact connected manifold admitting a continuous associative multiplication with identity and inverses is a topological group; since the underlying space is a finite-dimensional
manifold, Hilbert‚Äôs fifth theorem [11] implies that it is a Lie group. Simple-connectedness
ensures the group is its own universal cover, so the composition has no topological ambiguity. V3 is the DFD-specific bridge from the multiplicative vacuum-composition law in P1
to an internal topological carrier of that composition. It is well-motivated within DFD but
remains an axiom, not a tautology; in a different theory without multiplicative refractive
composition, V3 would not apply.
Axiom 4 (Stability). K is compact and simply-connected. Every irreducible factor in its
de Rham decomposition is Einstein with positive Ricci curvature.
Motivation. The Einstein condition Ric = Œª g (Œª > 0) is the critical point of the spectral
action at fixed volume‚Äîthe vacuum ground state. A non-Einstein K would be unstable
under Ricci flow and would relax to the Einstein point.
Axiom 5 (Minimality of dimension). dim(K) is the minimum compatible with Axioms 1‚Äì4
and the existence of chiral zero modes (nonzero spinc Dirac index).
Motivation. Each additional dimension introduces potential moduli that must be stabilized.
The minimal-dimension vacuum has the cleanest low-energy spectrum.
Axiom 6 (Minimality of topology). At fixed dimension, K has the minimum second Betti
number b2 (K) compatible with Axioms 1‚Äì5.

UNIQUENESS OF THE INTERNAL MANIFOLD

3

Motivation. Each independent 2-cycle supports an independent harmonic 2-form, hence
an independent gauge modulus. Minimizing b2 ensures the vacuum carries no unnecessary
internal flux degrees of freedom.
Remark 2.1 (Axiom content). Axioms 1‚Äì6 do not reference the Standard Model gauge group,
fermion representations, hypercharge assignments, or any coupling constant. Their physical
sources are:
‚Ä¢ V1: the spectral action framework‚Äîstandard in noncommutative geometry [2];
‚Ä¢ V2: parity violation‚Äîan empirical fact [3], combined with the mathematical requirement that the spinc Dirac index factorizes cleanly (KaÃàhler condition);
‚Ä¢ V3: fiber-wise consistency of the multiplicative vacuum-composition law n = eœà ‚Äîa
direct consequence of DFD‚Äôs postulate P1 combined with the spectral completion V1;
‚Ä¢ V4: vacuum ground-state stability‚Äîa standard physical requirement;
‚Ä¢ V5, V6: parsimony applied to dimension and topology‚Äîstandard in physics.
3. Derivation
3.1. Step 0: Product structure is forced.
Lemma 3.1 (Forced product decomposition). The factors required by Axioms 2 and 3 must
be distinct irreducible components of K. In particular, K is not irreducible.
Proof. All compact simply-connected Lie groups are parallelizable: they admit a global frame
of left-invariant vector fields, so T G ‚àº
= G √ó Rn is trivial. Therefore w1 (G) = w2 (G) = 0 for
any compact simply-connected Lie group G.
Axiom 2 requires w2 Ã∏= 0 on the KaÃàhler‚Äìchirality factor. Since w2 = 0 on any Lie group factor, the KaÃàhler‚Äìchirality factor cannot be the Lie group factor. They are distinct irreducible
components.
By Axiom 4, K is compact and simply-connected. The de Rham decomposition theorem [9]
guarantees that K splits uniquely (up to order) as a Riemannian product of irreducible
factors:
(2)

K = K1 √ó K2 √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó Kr .

At least one factor (call it KC ) satisfies Axiom 2, and at least one distinct factor (call it KG )
satisfies Axiom 3.
‚ñ°
3.2. Step 1: The chirality factor is CP 2 .
Lemma 3.2 (Classification of KaÃàhler‚Äìchirality factors). Let X be a compact, simply-connected,
KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein manifold with positive scalar curvature and w2 (X) Ã∏= 0. Then dimR (X) ‚â• 4,
and the unique such manifold of minimal real dimension with b2 = 1 is X = CP 2 .
Proof. Real dimension 2 (complex dimension 1). The compact simply-connected KaÃàhler 1folds are the Riemann surfaces of genus 0, i.e., CP 1 ‚àº
= S 2 . But S 2 is spin (w2 (S 2 ) = 0), so
it violates w2 Ã∏= 0. Excluded.
Real dimension 4 (complex dimension 2). The compact simply-connected KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein
surfaces with c1 > 0 are classified. By Tian [4] and Tian‚ÄìYau [5], the del Pezzo surfaces
admitting KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein metrics are determined by the Matsushima‚ÄìLichnerowicz obstruction [6, 7] (the automorphism group must be reductive) and the Tian‚ÄìYau Œ±-invariant:

The critical exclusion: CP 2 #CP 2 does not admit a KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein metric. This
is the Matsushima‚ÄìLichnerowicz theorem [6, 7]: a necessary condition for a compact KaÃàhler
manifold to admit a KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein metric with c1 > 0 is that its automorphism group
be reductive. The automorphism group Aut(F1 ) of F1 = CP 2 #CP 2 (the first Hirzebruch
surface) contains a unipotent radical and is therefore not reductive. No KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein
metric exists.
Remark 3.3 (Page metric exclusion). The Page metric [10] on CP 2 #CP 2 is Einstein but
not KaÃàhler. Axiom 2 requires KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein, so the Page metric is excluded at the axiom
level, not by post hoc selection.
The del Pezzo surfaces CP 2 #kCP 2 for k = 3, . . . , 8 do admit KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein metrics [4],
but they have b2 = k + 1 ‚â• 4, and are excluded by Axiom 6: CP 2 has b2 = 1, which is
strictly less.
Therefore CP 2 is the unique manifold satisfying Axiom 2 in real dimension 4 with minimal b2 .
‚ñ°
Corollary 3.4. The KaÃàhler‚Äìchirality factor in K is KC = CP 2 (dim = 4).
3.3. Step 2: The group factor is S 3 .
Lemma 3.5 (Classification of minimal Lie group factors). The unique compact simplyconnected Lie group of minimal positive dimension is SU(2) ‚àº
= S 3 (dim = 3).
Proof. The compact simply-connected Lie groups are classified by Cartan. Their dimensions
begin:

No compact simply-connected Lie group exists in dimension 1 (U(1) ‚àº

‚ñ°
Corollary 3.6. By Axioms 3 and 5, the Lie group factor is KG = S 3 (dim = 3).

5

3.4. Step 3: K = CP 2 √ó S 3 and nothing else.
Theorem 3.7 (Uniqueness of the internal manifold). Under Axioms 1‚Äì6, K = CP 2 √ó S 3 .
Proof. By Lemma 3.1, K has at least two irreducible factors: KC (satisfying Axiom 2) and
KG (satisfying Axiom 3).
By Corollary 3.4, KC = CP 2 (dimension 4).
By Corollary 3.6, KG = S 3 (dimension 3).
By Axiom 5 (minimize dim(K)):
(3)

dim(K) ‚â• dim(KC ) + dim(KG ) = 4 + 3 = 7.

Any additional irreducible factor K3 would satisfy dim(K3 ) ‚â• 1, giving dim(K) ‚â• 8 > 7,
violating minimality.
Could the chirality factor have dim > 4? The next KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein spinc -not-spin option
is CP 3 (dim = 6), giving dim(K) ‚â• 6 + 3 = 9 > 7. Excluded by Axiom 5.
Could the Lie group factor have dim > 3? The next option is SU(3) (dim = 8), giving
dim(K) ‚â• 4 + 8 = 12 > 7. Excluded by Axiom 5.
Could a non-product 7-manifold satisfy all axioms? No. Axiom 3 requires a Lie group
factor; Axiom 2 requires a KaÃàhler factor with w2 Ã∏= 0. All compact simply-connected Lie
groups are parallelizable, hence w2 = 0. These two requirements are incompatible on a single
connected manifold. The product decomposition K = KC √ó KG is forced (Lemma 3.1).
Verification of all axioms on K = CP 2 √ó S 3 :
‚Ä¢ V1: CP 2 √ó S 3 is compact, Riemannian, spinc ; the spectral action is well-defined. ‚úì
‚Ä¢ V2: CP 2 is KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein (Fubini‚ÄìStudy, Ric = 6g), with w2 = H Ã∏= 0. ‚úì
‚Ä¢ V3: S 3 ‚àº
= SU(2) is a compact simply-connected Lie group. ‚úì
‚Ä¢ V4: CP 2 is Einstein (Ric = 6g), S 3 is Einstein (Ric = 2g), both with positive Ricci
curvature. K is simply-connected (product of simply-connected spaces). ‚úì
‚Ä¢ V5: dim(K) = 7, the minimum possible. ‚úì
‚Ä¢ V6: b2 (K) = b2 (CP 2 )+b2 (S 3 ) = 1+0 = 1, the minimum possible for any K satisfying
V1‚ÄìV5. ‚úì
Chiral zero-mode existence (consistency check). The spinc Dirac index on CP 2 √ó S 3 is
nonzero. By KuÃànneth factorization [1, Theorem F.9], the index factors over the product.
With the canonical spinc structure on CP 2 and minimal-energy flux configuration on S 3 ,
the index equals 3, confirming chiral zero modes exist. This is a consistency check on the
derived manifold, not an input to the selection.
Therefore K = CP 2 √ó S 3 is the unique solution to Axioms 1‚Äì6.

5. Honest Assessment of Axiom Content
The six axioms do not reference the Standard Model, but they are not content-free:
‚Ä¢ V2 imports an empirical fact: parity violation. This is observational input,
not SM-theoretical input. Any vacuum theory must accommodate it. The KaÃàhler
requirement within V2 is motivated by the spectral action framework (V1): the index
computation factorizes cleanly in the KaÃàhler setting.
‚Ä¢ V3 is DFD-specific: V3 bridges the multiplicative vacuum-composition law in
P1 to an internal topological carrier of that composition via the fiber-wise consistency requirement of the spectral completion (V1). It is not a universal physical
requirement‚Äîit is specific to theories with n = eœà multiplicative refractive composition. Within DFD, it is well-motivated but remains an axiom, not a tautology.
‚Ä¢ V6 serves as a tiebreaker: V6 is invoked only to exclude del Pezzo surfaces with
k ‚â• 3 that admit KaÃàhler‚ÄìEinstein metrics. Without V6, CP 2 would still be the
preferred chirality factor by V5 (all competitors have the same dimension but more
topology), but V6 makes the exclusion explicit rather than implicit.
The derivation is not assumption-free. It is assumption-minimal : six qualitative physical
requirements produce a unique topological answer from which 30+ quantitative predictions
follow.
6. Conclusion
Theorem (Uniqueness of the internal manifold). Under Axioms V1‚ÄìV6, the internal manifold in the spectral completion of DFD is uniquely K = CP 2 √ó S 3 .
The key mathematical ingredients are:
(1) Incompatibility lemma: Lie groups are parallelizable (w2 = 0), so V2 (w2 Ã∏= 0)
and V3 (Lie group) force a product decomposition. This is derived, not assumed.
(2) Matsushima‚ÄìLichnerowicz obstruction: CP 2 #CP 2 does not admit a KaÃàhler‚Äì
Einstein metric, killing the only non-trivial competitor to CP 2 in dimension 4. This
is a classical theorem [6], not a new postulate.
(3) Cartan classification: SU(2) ‚àº
= S 3 is the unique compact simply-connected Lie
group of minimum positive dimension. This is textbook.
(4) Dimensional arithmetic: 4 + 3 = 7 leaves no room for additional factors.
Section XVIII.B.d of [1] stated: ‚ÄúThe only genuinely open theoretical question is the origin
of the CP 2 √ó S 3 topology itself.‚Äù

UNIQUENESS OF THE INTERNAL MANIFOLD

7

Under V1‚ÄìV6, the answer is: six physical requirements on the œà-vacuum‚Äîspectral completion, KaÃàhler chirality, multiplicative composition, ground-state stability, and two minimality
conditions‚Äîadmit exactly one solution. Whether those six axioms are themselves derivable
from deeper principles is a separate question; what this paper establishes is that they suffice
to uniquely determine K, eliminating the postulational status of the internal manifold.

WELL-POSEDNESS AND BOUNDARY VALUE PROBLEMS FOR A CLASS
OF QUASILINEAR DIVERGENCE-FORM EQUATIONS ARISING IN
DENSITY FIELD DYNAMICS
GARY ALCOCK

Abstract. We study the quasilinear elliptic partial differential equation

‚àí‚àá ¬∑ ¬µ(|‚àáœà|)‚àáœà = f in ‚Ñ¶ ‚äÜ R3 ,
where ¬µ is a nonlinear constitutive function. Motivated by density-field models of gravitational
optics, we develop a rigorous framework for existence, uniqueness, and regularity of weak solutions,
extend the analysis to exterior domains with asymptotically flat boundary conditions, and incorporate monotone nonlinear Robin‚ÄìNeumann conditions modeling photon-spheres and horizons. We
further establish stability estimates, continuous dependence on data, and parabolic well-posedness
using nonlinear semigroup theory. A variational formulation, catalog of admissible ¬µ-families, and
finite element method (FEM) implementation outline are provided. Open problems relevant to
global existence and singularity formation are discussed.

Contents
1. Introduction
Notation
1.1. Physical motivation for ¬µ and boundary conditions
2. Assumptions on ¬µ
3. Weak formulation and variational structure
4. Main results
5. Exterior domains and optical boundary conditions
6. Stability and continuous dependence
7. Parabolic extension and semigroup theory
8. Finite element method (FEM) implementation
9. Catalog of admissible ¬µ-families
10. Open problems
Acknowledgements
References

1. Introduction
We investigate the nonlinear elliptic equation

‚àí‚àá ¬∑ ¬µ(|‚àáœà|)‚àáœà = f,

(1)

posed on a domain ‚Ñ¶ ‚äÜ R3 . Here œà : ‚Ñ¶ ‚Üí R is the unknown scalar potential, ¬µ : [0, ‚àû) ‚Üí (0, ‚àû)
is a nonlinear coefficient, and f represents a source term. Such equations belong to the class of
quasilinear divergence-form PDEs with p-growth, generalizing the p-Laplacian. They arise in fluid

mechanics, nonlinear diffusion, and, in recent physical models, as optical potentials in effective
theories of gravitation.
Notation.
‚Ä¢ Lp (‚Ñ¶): standard Lebesgue spaces, 1 ‚â§ p ‚â§ ‚àû.
‚Ä¢ W 1,p (‚Ñ¶): Sobolev space of Lp functions with Lp weak derivatives.

1.1. Physical motivation for ¬µ and boundary conditions. In density-field models of gravitation, one introduces an ‚Äúoptical potential‚Äù œà such that the refractive index is n = eœà . The
flux coefficient ¬µ(|‚àáœà|) encodes the response of the medium to spatial gradients of œà. Its form
determines how weak-field Newtonian gravity, strong-field photon spheres, and effective horizon
behavior emerge.
Boundary conditions are motivated as follows:
‚Ä¢ Photon sphere: defined by an extremum of the optical circumference n(r)r. This yields
a Robin-type condition with coefficient Œ∫opt (œà) tied to the local optical speed.
‚Ä¢ Horizon: at the surface where outgoing null characteristics stall, one enforces an ‚Äúingoing
flux only‚Äù condition. Mathematically this corresponds to a nonlinear Neumann condition
eliminating outgoing flux. We emphasize this is physically motivated but mathematically
non-standard, and justifying it within elliptic PDE theory is an open problem.
2. Assumptions on ¬µ
We assume ¬µ : [0, ‚àû) ‚Üí (0, ‚àû) satisfies:
‚Ä¢ (A1) Continuity: ¬µ is continuous on [0, ‚àû).
‚Ä¢ (A2) Coercivity: ‚àÉŒ± > 0, p ‚â• 2 such that

5. Exterior domains and optical boundary conditions
Let ‚Ñ¶ = R3 \ BR denote an exterior domain. We impose:
‚Ä¢ Asymptotic flatness: œà(x) ‚Üí 0 as |x| ‚Üí ‚àû.
‚Ä¢ Photon-sphere boundary: Nonlinear Robin condition
a(‚àáœà) ¬∑ n + Œ∫opt (œà) œà = gph

‚Ä¢ Horizon boundary: Ingoing-flux Neumann condition

This asymmetric boundary condition is physically motivated but not standard in elliptic
PDE theory. A full mathematical justification remains open.
Theorem 5.1 (Exterior well-posedness). Under (A1)‚Äì(A4) and the above boundary conditions,
there exists a weak solution œà ‚àà W 1,p (‚Ñ¶). If the boundary operators are strictly monotone, the
solution is unique.
6. Stability and continuous dependence
Theorem 6.1 (Stability). Let œà1 , œà2 be solutions with data (f1 , BC1 ), (f2 , BC2 ). If a is strongly
monotone and locally Lipschitz, then

8. Finite element method (FEM) implementation
The weak form (2) is directly implementable in finite element packages. Nonlinear terms are
treated via Newton iteration with Jacobian

Remark 8.1. At
p |‚àáœà| ‚Üí 0, the Jacobian may become ill-conditioned. A practical remedy is to
replace |‚àáœà| by |‚àáœà|2 + s20 with small s0 > 0 (regularization). For background on FEM analysis
of quasilinear PDEs, see [8, 9].
Optical boundary conditions appear as Robin/Neumann integrals in the variational form.

Why Nuclear Clocks: The 229Th Annual Modulation Test of
Scalar-Field Gravitational Coupling
Gary Alcock
Independent Researcher

February 25, 2026

Abstract

Introduction

The thorium-229 nuclear isomer has a uniquely low
excitation energy of ‚àº 8.4 eV (ŒΩTh ‚âà 2,020,407 GHz),
accessible to VUV laser spectroscopy. Recent work
has established the absolute transition frequency to
kHz precision [1], resolved the five-line nuclear electric quadrupole structure [1], measured the thermal
sensitivity at three temperatures with line b shifting by only 5(5) kHz over 79 K [4], identified a
zero-shift operating temperature T0 = 196(5) K [2],
and demonstrated sub-kHz frequency reproducibility
across independent crystals [2]. The fine-structure

Precision atomic clock comparisons constrain pure

the DFD signal, leaving only the pure Œ±-sector
residual‚Äîwhich is indeed negligible.

In the ‚Äúfamily + clock‚Äù framework (Appendix T of
[6]), the coupling flows through nuclear and electronic family charges that cancel identically for sameion comparisons. The E3/E2 null does not constrain the cross-species signal. Same-ion composition
cancellation is a generic feature of any compositiondependent scalar theory; we present the E3/E2 consistency as a post-diction compatible with the framework rather than a pre-registered prediction.

2. Phase: Maximum at perihelion (January 3).

Thermal systematics. Higgins et al. [4] measured line b at three temperatures:
ŒΩb =
2,020,407,298.727(4) MHz at 150 K, 298.722(3) MHz
at 229 K, and 298.784(5) MHz at 293 K. Between
150 K and 229 K, line b shifts by only ‚àí5(5) kHz
over 79 K, with a zero-crossing near T0 = 196(5) K
as reported by Ooi et al. [2]. The second-order coefficient extracted from a quadratic fit to the three
points is b ‚âà 0.005 kHz/K2 . A seasonal cryostat
drift of ‚àÜT = 0.1 K at T0 therefore contributes
Œ¥ŒΩthermal ‚âà b(‚àÜT )2 ‚âà 0.05 Hz‚Äîa factor of ‚àº 160,000
below the strong-sector prediction of 8 kHz.

4. Crystal independence: Different 229 Th:CaF2
crystals (and, if available, 229 ThF4 thin
films [11]) should show identical fractional modulation, since the effect is nuclear.
5. Sign: Th-229 frequency increases at perihelion
(deeper potential ‚Üí larger œà ‚Üí nuclear energy
shifts upward for KTh > 0).
6. GR prediction: Exactly zero. Any nonzero
composition-dependent annual modulation in
the Th-229/Sr ratio is new physics.

Confrontation
Data

4.1

with

Co-thermometry diagnostic.

=

giving a thermal coefficient ‚àº 40√ó larger than line b
near T0 . Measuring lines b and c simultaneously provides a sharp diagnostic: if an apparent annual signal in line b is accompanied by a 40√ó larger signal
in line c at the same phase, the origin is thermal.
If line c shows no annual modulation (or modulation at the DFD-predicted fractional level, identical
to line b), the origin is gravitational.

Ooi et al. 2026: existing constraint
and reanalysis target

Ooi et al. [2] report line b center frequencies at 195 K
across crystals C10 and C13 over 7 months, with
weighted mean ŒΩb = 2,020,407,298,701.18 kHz, standard error 0.22 kHz, and œáÃÉ2 = 0.4.

Reanalysis protocol. Fit the measurement dates
and center frequencies to:

Existing constraint on annual modulation.
The œáÃÉ2 = 0.4 indicates the data are more consistent
than expected from statistical scatter alone‚Äîthere is
no excess variance that could accommodate a multikHz annual signal. Over a 7-month window, the orbital cosine swings by ‚â≥ 1.25 of its full range, making
the data sensitive to annual modulation. The 220 Hz
reproducibility and absence of drift constrain:

Relation to Other BSM Searches

Annual modulation in clock ratios is not unique
to DFD. Any scalar-field theory coupling to the
Standard Model‚Äîincluding ultralight dark matter
(ULDM) models and varying-Œ± frameworks [9, 10]‚Äî
can produce similar signals. What distinguishes
DFD:

If the signal lies near the Ooi upper bound (‚àº
200 Hz), it becomes detectable at ‚àº 5œÉ with ‚àº 100 Hz
per-measurement precision and ‚àº 12 measurements.
The composition-coupling floor (‚àº 26 Hz) requires
‚àº 10 Hz per-measurement precision, corresponding
to fractional uncertainty of ‚àº 5 √ó 10‚àí15 ‚Äîa target
for mature nuclear clock technology expected within
several years.

1. Channel structure:
DFD‚Äôs compositiondependent coupling model is consistent with
the PTB E3/E2 null (composition cancellation)
while predicting that Th-229/Sr should show a
signal (strong-sector coupling). Most ULDM
models predict universal Œ±-coupling and do not
naturally explain the E3/E2 null.
6
